EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if Contractor 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease. 
If the contractor maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be 
entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the contractor.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the Contractor; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 
20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance 
as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-
insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or agents shall be 
excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

Waiver of Subrogation
Contractor hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said Contractor 
may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  Contractor 
agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of subrogation, but this 
provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of subrogation 
endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the Contractor to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof of 
ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within the 
retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
Contractor shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies of 
the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

SUBJECT:
Fiscal Year 2023-24 General Liability and Property Insurance – Purchase Authorization
RECOMMENDATION:
Authorize a staff committee comprised of the City Manager, the Finance Director, and the City Attorney to select the most 
cost-effective program to insure the City's general liability and property risks and authorize the City Attorney to execute all 
required documents to purchase the selected insurance for fiscal year 2023-24.
BACKGROUND:
For the past three years, Council authorized a staff committee comprised of the City Manager, the Finance Director, and 
the City Attorney to select the most cost-effective program to insure the City’s general liability and property risk, and 
authorized the City Attorney to execute all required documents to purchase the selected insurance. The request for 
Council to authorize a committee in fiscal year 2020-21 was to allow the City to enroll in an organization called Public Risk 
Innovations, Solutions, and Management (PRISM), a joint powers authority comprised of dozens of government agencies, 
that provided coverage up to $25 million above a $1 million Self-Insured Retention (SIR). 
The City currently faces an extremely challenging insurance market, similar to last year. The fluid circumstances of excess 
liability and property coverage renewal are currently in review by the staff committee. In May 2023, the City Attorney’s 
Office received the fiscal year 2023-24 Insurance Budget Estimates and PRISM premium estimates, and then received 
updated estimates for renewal premiums in June 2023, as follows: 
Fiscal Year 2023-24 Insurance Premium Estimates

As noted above, the estimated premium for Excess Liability is $1,038,772, which is lower than the budget low estimate of 
$1,064,000 but still represents a 32.7% increase.  Further, the estimated premium for Property, Boiler & Machinery, 
Environmental & Cyber coverage at $1,024,773 is slightly less than the budget high estimate of $1,034,809 but represents 
a sizable 45.1% increase. Also, the estimated premium for Property (Bridges) coverage at $121,624 is lower than the 
budget low estimate of $125,949. While renewal premiums for sexual abuse and molestation (SAM) as well as the San 
Mateo-Foster City Public Financing Authority (PFA) are yet to be determined, staff expects these to total less than $40,000.  
Coverage for the PFA renews in September 2023, and the final renewal quote will be provided to staff in approximately 1-2 
months. 
The renewal premium estimates currently total $2,268,055, which is below the budget high estimated total of $2,430,031.  
Given the significant increases in total premiums year over year, the staff committee is considering whether to explore the 
feasibility of becoming a self-insured public entity.  This will require research and careful review of the City’s options, and a 
study session would occur before any proposed decision is presented to the Council.  
The staff committee is recommending that Council authorize the committee to select the most cost-effective solution 
continuing into fiscal year 2023-24 as circumstances remain challenging and unusual. The City Attorney would then 
execute all of the documents necessary to obtain that insurance. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The City paid approximately $1.78 million for general liability and property insurance (including bridges, cyber, and crime) 
for fiscal year 2022-23. The renewal premium for 2023-24, excluding liability for SAM and the PFA is $2,268,055. The 
proposed fiscal year 2023-24 budget assumed $2.43 million for insurance premiums, therefore no modifications to 
appropriations are required.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in 
the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)

SUBJECT:
Downtown Parking Equipment Network Hosting and Materials – Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 2 to the agreement with IPS Group, Inc. for parking equipment network hosting and materials to 
increase the agreement amount by $275,000 for a revised agreement total of $675,000 and extend the agreement for an 
additional three-year term ending June 30, 2026; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in 
substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The Downtown Parking Technology project resulted in the installation of 119 multi-space pay stations and 386 single-space 
parking meters for the Downtown paid parking system. IPS Group, Inc. installed the equipment and the City contracts with 
them for hosting the network gateway and providing materials for maintenance as needed.  
In June 2021, Council approved an agreement in the amount of $300,000 with IPS Group, Inc. to provide the monthly 
network hosting services required by the City’s Downtown parking revenue equipment, as well as for providing proprietary 
parts and materials on an as-needed basis to maintain the equipment in good working condition. This equipment includes 
all the parking meters and pay stations for the Downtown parking program. The funding for the initial agreement also 
included payment of the credit card transaction fees for each user transaction utilizing credit card payment.
In April 2023, City Council approved Amendment No. 1 to the agreement increasing the agreement amount by $100,000 to 
provide enough funding for additional credit card transaction fees through the original agreement duration. The original 
agreement expires on June 30, 2023.
Per the fiscal year 2023-24 Comprehensive Fee Schedule, adopted by City Council on May 1, 2023, the credit card provider 
will charge the credit card transaction fee to users beginning July 1, 2023. This amendment will extend the term of the 
agreement for another three-year term and add funds to the agreement for network hosting and materials for that 
timeframe. The total not-to-exceed amount for the additional term of this agreement has taken this reduction in costs into 
account.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding for these services is budgeted in the Downtown Services Operations budget.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This contract amendment is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that 
will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)

SUBJECT:
Hillsdale Caltrain Commuter Shuttle – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a two-year agreement with Commute.org (Peninsula Traffic Congestion Relief Alliance) for the Hillsdale Caltrain 
Commuter Shuttle in the amount of $83,132 and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in 
substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
Historically, the City has partnered with Commute.org, and previously the Peninsula Joint Powers Board, to provide 
commuter shuttle bus service to major business parks and destinations within San Mateo. The latest agreement for 
operations was approved by Council on July 18, 2022, for shuttle bus service on two routes (Campus Drive and Norfolk 
Area) that connect the City's Caltrain stations with the major business parks. The agreement updated the management 
and operations structure for the shuttle program and updated the mutual funding responsibilities for Commute.org and 
the City. Due to service disruptions as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, related local health orders, and the reduced 
number of commuters utilizing shuttle services, the Norfolk shuttle route was on hold for the last three years. The current 
agreement with Commute.org for this service ends effective June 30, 2023.
Earlier this year, Commute.org applied for grant funding through the San Mateo County Transportation Authority’s 
(SMCTA) call for projects for continued operation of the City’s shuttle routes. Due to changes in the grant structure, the 
shuttle team proposed revising the existing shuttle routes to a new split route (Hillsdale Commuter Caltrain HCC East and 
West). The new route was designed to focus on the stops with high ridership (primarily the San Mateo Medical Center, 
Alameda/20th, and office buildings near Concar and Amphlett). The SMCTA approved the grant application for the Hillsdale 
Commuter Caltrain shuttle route, and this agreement formalizes the terms and conditions for operations.
As in prior years, Commute.org will contribute 75 percent of the total cost and the City will be responsible for the 
remaining 25 percent of the cost. Given the shuttle route changes, City staff will coordinate with Commute.org to provide 
community updates ahead of the July 3rd service start. This agreement is for a two-year term, in accordance with the 
funding agreement between Commute.org and SMCTA.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The total two-year agreement cost for shuttle operations is $332,526. Commute.org’s maximum contribution is 75 percent 
of the total cost, or $249,394. The City is responsible for the remaining portion of the total cost, equating to approximately 
25 percent of the total cost, or $83,132, for the two-year term. There is sufficient funding in the Citywide Parking and 
Transportation budget for the City’s share of costs.

SHUTTLE BUS SERVICE ADMINISTRATION AGREEMENT 
HILLSDALE CALTRAIN COMMUTER 
 
This Shuttle Bus Service Administration Agreement ("Agreement") is entered into this 
________2023 ("Effective Date"), between the City of San Mateo ("Lead Organization"), and 
Commute.org formerly Peninsula Traffic Congestion Relief Alliance. For clarity, references to 
"the Parties" mean the Lead Organization and Commute.org.   
RECITALS 
A. 
Lead Organization desires to participate in a shuttle bus service ("Shuttle Service") 
as part of the Shuttle Bus Program ("Program").  
B. 
The purpose of this Agreement is to set forth the terms and conditions of Lead 
Organization's proposed participation in the Shuttle Service and in the Program. 
AGREEMENT 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, the Parties agree as 
follows: 
1. 
Authorization.  Subject to the terms and conditions set forth below, Lead 
Organization hereby is authorized to participate in the Program.  The Hillsdale Caltrain Commuter 
(“Shuttle”) shall operate to and from the Hillsdale Caltrain Station, in San Mateo, California, and 
employment and residential areas along the route shown on the map attached to this Agreement as 
Exhibit A, and described in the schedule attached to this Agreement as Exhibit B.  The route and 
frequency of the Shuttle may be reasonably modified by Commute.org from time to time.  Lead 
Organization understands and agrees that more than one participant may be served by the Shuttle.   
2. 
Lead Organization.  Lead Organization acknowledges that it has been selected and 
accepts the position of Lead Organization on behalf of all organizations served by the Shuttle.  As 
Lead Organization, Lead Organization shall provide the projected minimum Twenty-Five Percent 
(25.0%) financial share, excluding contributions from Commute.org described under Section 5 of 
the Agreement, Maximum Contribution; Payment. 
Lead Organization may seek appropriate contributions from participating organizations for 
reimbursement of amounts expended by Lead Organization hereunder, it being understood that 
Lead Organization shall not seek or make a profit from serving as Lead Organization.  
Commute.org has no responsibility to collect any such amounts from such other organizations 
participating in the Program to be serviced by the Shuttle. 
3. 
Shuttle Service Vendor.  Shuttle Service is operated by the San Mateo County 
Transit District's Shuttle Provider (“Vendor”) in full accordance with the terms and conditions of 
72 of 536

--- page 73 ---
the contract between SamTrans and the Vendor. Commute.org or its designee will have daily 
management responsibilities for the Shuttle.  
 
Commute.org shall be responsible for supplying and/or completing any grant-related 
reporting information.  Lead Organization shall assist Commute.org in obtaining appropriate 
reporting information. 
 
4. 
Term.  The term of this Agreement shall commence on July 1, 2023 and end on 
June 30, 2025.  Any obligations which are incurred under the Agreement which are expressly 
provided to extend beyond the term of this Agreement, including any contribution obligations 
incurred by Commute.org pursuant to Section 5, shall remain valid until fulfilled as provided for 
in this Agreement.  This Agreement may be terminated by either party without cause by giving 
thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other in the manner provided below.   
All subsidy funds provided for the Program shall be generated from non-Commute.org 
revenue streams.  At no time shall Commute.org be liable for additional revenue not included as 
part of the external authorized grant or for expenses disallowed by the granting entities (grantor). 
Commute.org budget decisions are subject to the discretion of Commute.org's Executive Director 
and Commute.org's Board of Directors.  This Agreement will terminate without penalty, liability 
or expense of any kind to Commute.org, if funds are not appropriated for the current fiscal year to 
fund the Shuttle Services.  If funds are appropriated for a portion of the current fiscal year, this 
Agreement will terminate without penalty, liability or expense of any kind to Commute.org, at the 
end of the term for which the funds are appropriated.  If expected revenues, from pending or future 
grant applications, which are intended to fulfill any portion of Commute.org contribution under 
this Agreement, are not approved and/or received by Commute.org, this Agreement will terminate 
without penalty, liability or expense of any kind to Commute.org, at the end of the term for which 
the funds are appropriated.  Commute.org has no obligation to make appropriations for this 
Agreement in lieu of appropriations for new or other agreements.   
 
The term of this Agreement may be extended to cover additional funding periods, by 
mutual written agreement.  In the event that the Agreement term is extended, the Parties shall set 
forth any terms, including Commute.org's maximum contribution that will be applicable during 
the period of extension. 
5. 
Maximum Contribution; Payment.  Commute.org's maximum contribution (which 
includes contributions from all other funding agencies involved, if any) for Shuttle Service shall 
not exceed Two Hundred and Forty-Nine Thousand Three Hundred Ninety-Four Dollars 
($249,394) or Seventy-Five point (75.0%) of the estimated total costs for the Shuttle Service as 
determined by Commute.org whichever amount is less.   
6. 
Lead Organization's Payment.  Lead Organization shall pay Twenty-Five Percent 
(25.0%) of the total actual costs of the Shuttle Service described in Section 1 of this Agreement.  
Lead Organization's 25.0% share shall equal Eighty-Three Thousand One Hundred Thirty-Two 
($83,132), subject to a final adjustment as provided below.   
 
Lead Organization shall pay based on quarterly invoices from Commute.org.  Lead 
Organization shall pay each invoice within thirty (30) days from the date of the invoice.  In the

Commute.org with any reporting requirements related to the performance of this Agreement.  
Commute.org shall bear its own costs connected with such reporting.  Lead Organization shall 
only be responsible for its own costs for making available all records relating to the Lead 
Organization’s participation in the Program in the event such a report is requested.  Records may 
include, but are not limited to ridership reports, rider surveys, customer compliments/complaints, 
schedule variants and effective dates. 
 
9. 
Marketing Program.  The Commute.org countywide shuttle marketing program 
description will be attached and made a part of this agreement as Exhibit C – MARKETING 
PROGRAM.  Any brochures, handbooks, articles, news releases, timetables, web content, or other 
publications regarding the Shuttle (collectively “Collateral”) if provided by Lead Organization, 
shall occur at its sole expense with no additional reimbursement from Commute.org.  All such 
Collateral must be approved by Commute.org in advance of publication.  All Collateral shall 
identify the grantors as funding sources in a manner approved by Commute.org, with copies to 
Commute.org.  See Exhibit D – GRANTING ENTITIES.  During the term of this Agreement, 
funding sources may change.  Commute.org will notify the Lead Organization of such changes 
and any additional marketing requirements that may apply as a result.  The updates will not require 
Lead Organization consent.  Lead Organization shall comply with update for newly drafted 
marketing materials.

subcontractors, representatives or agents.  Commute.org will notify Lead Organization promptly

the Indemnitees will cooperate reasonably in the defense by providing information related to the 
Shuttle that is within their control.  Except in the event of sole negligence on the part of

and pay all charges of attorneys and all other costs and expenses arising therefrom or incurred in 
connection therewith; and if any judgment be rendered against the Indemnitees in any such action, 
Lead Organization shall, at its expense, satisfy and discharge the same. This indemnity shall 
survive the termination of this Agreement and or any extension thereof.

directors, officers, agents and employees (collectively, "Lead Organization Indemnitees") against

agents.  Lead Organization will notify Commute.org promptly of any claim, Commute.org will

Commute.org shall, at its expense, satisfy and discharge the same. This indemnity shall survive 
the termination of this Agreement and or any extension thereof.

EXHIBIT C 
MARKETING PROGRAM 
(As of July 1, 2023) 
 
Commute.org has developed a countywide shuttle marketing plan that serves as the basis for 
promoting the shuttle program throughout San Mateo County. Commute.org and its partners use 
a variety of tools to create and execute focused marketing for the shuttle program. 
 
The Commute.org website serves as a primary source of information about the shuttle program. 
Riders, and potential riders, use the site to get information about routes and schedules as well as 
real-time vehicle location tracking. The mobile version of the Commute.org site is optimized to 
provide shuttle riders with streamlined access to the information they need during their commute. 
 
Commute.org maintains and publishes route specific GTFS data that is used by transit planning 
apps including those managed by Google, Apple, 511 and app developers. Passengers can plan 
trips that include shuttles anywhere Commute.org shuttles operate. Route and schedule 
information is also available in hard-copy format. 
 
Commute.org works with the cities and property owners where shuttles operate to install and 
maintain signage indicating where the shuttles stop. New signage is being installed at stop 
locations throughout San Mateo County.  
 
Commute.org shuttle and employer outreach staff provide presentations about the shuttle 
program directly to employers and their employees as well as at community events. Rider 
appreciation events are conducted throughout the year and allow the Commute.org team to thank 
shuttle riders for their commitment to using shuttles for first/last mile transportation.

SUBJECT:
Construction Management Services for the Clean Water Program – Amendments 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve amendments to each of the professional services agreements with Tanner Pacific, Inc. and Townsend 
Management, Inc. to continue providing as-needed construction management services for the Clean Water Program 
wastewater infrastructure improvement projects; increase the agreement amounts by $2,000,000 each for new total 
agreement amounts not to exceed $5,000,000 for each firm; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the 
amendments in substantially the form presented and to approve future amendments within the revised budget. 
BACKGROUND:
The Clean Water Program (CWP) includes improvements to wastewater system capacity, flow management, condition 
improvements, and inflow and infiltration reduction to comply with regulatory requirements. Construction management 
services provide as-needed support for the remainder of the Clean Water Program improvement projects, which include 
various new capacity relief sewers, sewer rehabilitations/repairs, lift station improvements, and improvements to the 
wastewater treatment plant (WWTP, officially the Water Quality Control Plant) facilities that are not scoped for 
replacement under the active WWTP Upgrade and Expansion contract. Construction management is a professional service 
that provides the City with effective management and inspection of the construction contractor’s project schedule, cost, 
quality, safety, and scope. 
On June 21, 2021, City Council approved agreements with Tanner Pacific, Inc. (Tanner Pacific) and Townsend Management, 
Inc. (Townsend) to provide as-needed construction management services for the various CWP collection system projects 
with agreements in amounts not-to-exceed $3,000,000 each. On August 15, 2022, City Council approved amendments 
(Amendments No. 1) to both agreements to expand the scope to include construction management services for WWTP 
projects, as well as to extend the durational term of the agreements from three to five years. At the time, no additional 
budget was added into the agreements. 
Since execution of the agreements, both Tanner Pacific and Townsend have been successfully providing construction 
management and inspection services on various CWP projects. Additional construction management and inspection 
services are required for upcoming sanitary sewer rehabilitation projects, pump station improvements, sewer pipe 
capacity improvements, and WWTP improvements required to improve safety and reliability of the existing facilities. These 
projects are anticipated to occur through the end of the five-year terms of the agreements.
The proposed amendments will increase the previously approved $3,000,000 agreement amounts by $2,000,000 to new 
not-to-exceed totals of $5,000,000 each for the Tanner Pacific and Townsend agreements to cover the additional 
construction management support needs. 
Prior to obtaining construction management and inspection services on any construction contract, staff obtains proposals 
87 of 536

--- page 88 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
from either of the two firms and negotiates a reasonable fee for the scope and expertise required. This practice will 
continue with each new project initiating construction.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds in the various Clean Water Program projects available to fund these amendments. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The City prepared a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) in accordance with the California
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) (SCH No. 2015032006). The PEIR was certified by Council on June 6, 2016. In accordance
with CEQA, the Final PEIR serves as the primary environmental compliance document for implementation of the Clean
Water Program. The City prepared Addenda to the Final PEIR that were approved on July 16, 2018 (Addendum 1) and April
15, 2019 (Addendum 2). This work is within the scope of the project evaluated in the PEIR and Addendum. 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Att 2 – Amendment No. 2 (Townsend Management)

AMENDMENT NO. 2 TO THE AGREEMENT
BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO AND TANNER PACIFIC, INC. 
FOR CLEAN WATER PROGRAM COLLECTION SYSTEM CONSTRUCTION 
MANAGEMENT SERVICES
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo (“City”), a municipal corporation of the State 
of California, and Tanner Pacific, Inc. (“Consultant”), entered into an Agreement for 
Clean Water Program Collection System Construction Management Services 
(“Agreement”) on July 1, 2021; and
WHEREAS, additional construction management and inspections services are 
needed for upcoming Clean Water Program Wastewater Treatment Plant and Collection 
System improvement projects; and
WHEREAS, City and Consultant wish to amend the Agreement effective July 1, 
2023 to increase the payment for professional services from $3,000,000 to a not to exceed 
amount of $5,000,000. 
NOW, THEREFORE, the parties agree as follows:
1.
The second sentence of Section 8 “Payment” of the Agreement is amended 
as follows: 
“In consideration for full performances of the services and any 
reimbursable expenses set forth in this Agreement, CITY agrees to pay 
CONSULTANT a fee based on verified time and materials not to exceed 
$5,000,000.”
2.
The remaining terms of the Agreement remain in full force and effect.

SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant: Grit, Rag, and Scum Hauling and Disposal – Bid Rejection 
and Contract Change Order
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to reject all bids received for the fiscal year 2023-24 Wastewater Treatment Plant grit, rag, and scum 
hauling and disposal services; approve Contract Change Order No. 1 with Allied Waste of North America, LLC dba Allied 
Waste Services of Daly City/Republic Services of Daly City for these services to extend the contract term to September 30, 
2023 and increase the contract amount by $41,000, for a new total contract amount of $163,760; and authorize the Public 
Works Director to execute the contract change order in substantially the form presented.  
BACKGROUND:
The Wastewater Treatment Plant (officially the Water Quality Control Plant) requires hauling and disposal of grit, rags, and 
scum on a weekly basis, which is essential to maintain normal operations. In accordance with Municipal Code section 
3.60.030, a competitive public process was used to obtain bids for this service. Specifications and a Notice Inviting Sealed 
Bids were published in April 2023. Three bids were received and opened on May 11, 2023, with the following results: 
Bid Amount
Contractor
Per Ton
Trip
Total
Compactor Management Company LLC
$63
$455
$119,350.00
Allied Waste of North America, LLC dba 
Allied Waste Services of Daly City/Republic 
$79
$505
$168,350.00
Advanced Chemical Transport, INC. DBA 
$135
$1,500
$340,500.00
During the bid review process, it was determined that there were discrepancies in the bid book; specifically, with the unit 
price per ton and round-trip transportation estimates. These discrepancies created inconsistent final bid amounts from 
each of the bidders. Pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.040(a)(4), the City Council, at its discretion, may 
reject all bids received. Therefore, in order to have a more uniform bidding process, it is recommended for City Council to 
reject all bids received and authorize the Director of Public Works to rebid the grit, rag, and scum hauling and disposal 
contract. 
The current agreement with Allied Waste Services of Daly City/Republic Services of Daly City for these services ends June 
30, 2023. The proposed contract change order extends the current agreement an additional three months to September 
30, 2023 and increases the amount by $41,000 for a new total agreement amount of $163,760. Change Order No. 1 will 
cover the cost to provide the removal of an estimated 320 tons of material over the additional three-month extended term 
during the anticipated rebid process for the new contract. 
91 of 536

--- page 92 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Based on the above, staff recommends for City Council to adopt a resolution rejecting all bids thereby allowing staff to 
rebid these services, and approve Contract Change Order No. 1 with Allied Waste Services of Daly City/Republic Services of 
Daly City extending the contract for three months and increasing the amount of the contract by $41,000, for a new total 
amount of $163,760. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding for grit, rag, and scum hauling and disposal services is budgeted in the Wastewater Treatment Plant’s operating 
budget.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This resolution and contract change order is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of 
the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private 
structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

RESOLUTION NO. ___ (2023)
REJECTING ALL BIDS FOR THE WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT GRIT, RAG, AND SCUM HAULING AND 
DISPOSAL SERVICES AND AUTHORIZING STAFF TO RE-BID THE PROJECT
WHEREAS, the Wastewater Treatment Plant (officially the Water Quality Control Plant) requires hauling 
and disposal of grit, rags, and scum on a weekly basis that is essential to maintain normal operations; and
WHEREAS, the City advertised the project through the competitive bid process in accordance with 
Section 3.60.040 of the Municipal Code; and 
WHEREAS, the City received and opened three bids on May 11, 2023 and the lowest bidder appeared to 
be Compactor Management, Inc. with a bid of $119,350.00; and
WHEREAS, during the bid review process it was determined that there were discrepancies in the bid 
book, specifically with the unit price per ton and round-trip transportation estimates, that created inconsistent 
final bid amounts from each of the bidders; and 
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.040(a)(4) allows the City Council to, at its discretion, 
reject all bids received, negotiate a contract for the work or supplies if the project is not within budget, do the 
work through City employees, or terminate the project or purchase; and
WHEREAS, it is in the City’s best interest to re-bid the project to remedy the discrepancies; and
WHEREAS, staff recommends the rejection of all bids and the authority to rebid the project. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that:
1.
This resolution is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the 
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public 
or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving 
negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)  
2.
In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.040(a)(4), all bids received on May 11, 
2023, for the Wastewater Treatment Plant grit, rag, and scum hauling and disposal services are 
hereby rejected.
3.
City staff is authorized to re-bid the project.

SUBJECT:
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project - Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 5 to the agreement with Wilsey Ham for engineering services during construction in an amount 
not to exceed $45,000 for a new agreement total of $573,874; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the 
amendment in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
In September 2021, City Council approved an agreement with Wilsey Ham to provide design services for the Citywide 
Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project (Project) in the amount of $354,000, with a contingency reserve in the amount of 
$41,000.  Amendments No. 1, 2, and 3 to the agreement were issued for additional design services on the Project utilizing 
available contingency and consistent with the authority granted by City Council. Amendments No. 1, 2, and 3 utilized 
$40,874 of the contingency amount with $126 of the contingency remaining. On July 18, 2022, City Council approved 
Amendment No. 4 in the amount of $134,000 to add engineering services during construction for the Project, for a total 
agreement amount of $528,874. 
Road reconstruction is currently underway and additional, unforeseen areas of base failures (soft soil) were discovered on 
Guildford Avenue after grinding away the existing road section. Additional base failure repairs are required to strengthen 
the subgrade prior to reconstructing the roadway. This work is observed in the field by Wilsey Ham’s geotechnical 
subconsultant, Furgo, in order to determine the extent and depth of the base repairs. The additional in-field inspection 
work is unforeseen and utilized the entire original budget for this task and additional budget to Wilsey Ham’s engineering 
services during construction contract is required. Due to the nature of base failure repairs, the full extent of the in-field 
inspection services is unknown until the base repair work is complete.
Based on the above, staff recommends that a not-to-exceed amount of $45,000 be added to the agreement to cover 
additional in-field geotechnical inspection services, for a total agreement amount of $573,874. Staff estimates the 
additional budget will be sufficient to complete the base repairs on Guilford Avenue and other potential unforeseen site 
conditions that arise through the completion of the Project. Construction is anticipated to be completed in August 2023.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the Citywide Street Reconstruction Package 1/Phase 3 Project (467771). 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
95 of 536

Guidelines Section 15301.)

EXHIBIT A-5
SCOPE OF SERVICES
PROJECT DESCRIPTION
CONSULTANT is providing Engineering Services During Construction (ESDC) for the Citywide Street 
Reconstruction Project, Phase 3 which is part of the City’s Smooth Streets Program (SSP). During 
construction, differing site conditions were encountered and the City desires to expand the 
CONSULTANT’s scope of services to include additional field site visits. 
SCOPE OF WORK
Task 1 –Construction Services
The following additional work is included in this task:
•
Attend one construction meeting with the City and contractor.
•
Perform proof rolling evaluation of pavement (up to 3 days assumed)
•
Review and respond to submittals (up to 2 submittals assumed).
•
Review and respond to Requests for Information (up to 2 RFI’s assumed).
•
Design minor revisions as necessary to accommodate field conditions.
•
Perform final walk-through inspection and prepare punch list. Prepare record drawings.
•
Geotechnical field observation services (up to 5, eight-hour field visits) and preparation of Daily 
Field Reports.
Deliverables: Responses to RFI’s in PDF format. Sketches and plan revisions, as required by field 
conditions. Contractor change orders, as necessary. Daily Field Reports provided by Fugro 
(geotechnical sub-consultant). 
Assumptions and Exclusions
The following assumptions and exclusions were used in the preparation of this proposal:
1. All permit costs and agency/utility fees will be paid by others.
2. CONSULTANT will submit earned value analysis (summary of hours and work performed by 
classification) when submitting monthly invoicing.
3. Any design coordination meetings will be attended virtually (video or phone).
4. Items not included in the scope above are excluded.
SCHEDULE
CONSULTANT will provide the scope of services described above on a mutually agreeable schedule.
BUDGET
CONSULTANT proposes to provide services on a time and materials basis with a not-to-exceed budget 
of $45,000 in accordance with the 2023 City of San Mateo Fee Schedule, effective through December 
31, 2023.

SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action Projects, Package II – Appropriation 
and Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to appropriate $75,000 from Fund 72 reserves to the Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action 
Projects, Package II project (IAP-II); approve Amendment No. 10 to the agreement with Stantec Consulting Services, Inc. to 
provide professional design and engineering services during construction for IAP-II; increase the contract value by $75,000, 
for a revised total agreement amount not to exceed $1,727,403.46; extend the term through December 31, 2023; and 
authorize the Public Works Director to execute Amendment No. 10 in substantially the form presented and approve future 
amendments within the approved amount. 
BACKGROUND:
The Clean Water Program (CWP) includes significant infrastructure upgrades at the Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP, 
officially the Water Quality Control Plant). The Immediate Action Projects, Package II (IAP-II) includes scope to improve the 
aging solids handling systems at the WWTP.  In August 2017, the City executed a professional design services agreement in 
the amount of $876,000 with Stantec Consulting Services, Inc. (Stantec) for IAP-II. Amendment No. 1 was executed in 
March 2019, adding $517,930 for additional design services and engineering services during construction (ESDC). 
Amendment No. 2, executed in May 2021, added $79,800 for additional ESDC due to delays in the construction schedule 
and extended the agreement through December 31, 2021. 
The IAP-II project continues to experience multiple challenges related to installation of a new sludge cake storage hopper 
and two new sludge cake pumps, leading to additional construction delays resulting in an extended timeframe during 
which Stantec's ESDC are necessary. Multiple contract amendments have been executed to allow continued support from 
Stantec. Amendment No. 3, executed in December 2021, extended the term of Stantec’s agreement through March 31, 
2022 at no additional cost. Amendment No. 4 further extended the term for an additional month to allow time to bring 
Amendment No. 5 to City Council, which added $88,673.46 for additional ESDC and extended the term through September 
15, 2022. Amendment No. 6 added $30,000 for ESDC and extended the term through December 31, 2022. Amendment No. 
7 extended the term through January 2023 at no additional cost and Amendment No. 8 added $60,000 for ESDC and 
extended the term through April 2023. Amendment 9 extended the term through June 2023 at no additional cost. 
Amendment No. 10 provides $75,000 of additional budget for Stantec to provide ESDC for an additional six months 
through December 31, 2023, which is currently the anticipated timeframe to bring the project to conclusion. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Approval of Amendment No. 10 will increase Stantec’s not-to-exceed agreement amount by $75,000. An appropriation of 
$75,000 from unassigned fund balance from the Sewer Fund reserves to the IAP-II project (46t009) account is required.  
There are sufficient funds available in the Sewer Fund reserves for this appropriation. 
100 of 536

--- page 101 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The City prepared a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) in accordance with the California 
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) (SCH No. 2015032006). Council on June 6, 2016, certified the PEIR. In accordance with 
CEQA, the Final PEIR serves as the primary environmental compliance document for the implementation of the Clean 
Water Program. The City prepared Addenda to the Final PEIR that was approved on July 16, 2018 (Addendum 1) and April 
15, 2019 (Addendum 2). This work is within the scope of the Project evaluated in the PEIR and Addendum.

All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Uniform Maintenance Services – Contract Change Order
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure and approve Change Order No. 2 to the City’s 
agreement with Cintas Inc. for the purpose of uniform maintenance services in the amount of $20,000.01, for a new not to 
exceed total of $120,000.00 for fiscal year 2022-23; and authorize the City Manager to execute Change Order No. 2 in 
substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The City has a contract with Cintas, Inc. (Cintas) for uniform maintenance services. Originally, the City piggybacked on an 
existing agreement between Prince William County Public Schools and Cintas Inc. through OMNIA Partners, a cooperative 
purchasing organization, in February 2021, to provide uniform maintenance services for the Public Works and Parks & 
Recreation Departments. The City has received uniform maintenance services from Cintas since fiscal year 2011-12. The 
annual contract amount is up to the City Manager's level of contract authority, which is an amount not to exceed 
$100,000. Year to date, the City has paid Cintas $98,309.15. Based on service use trends to date, the City requires an 
additional $20,000.01 in contract authority for the remainder of fiscal year 2022-23. In the past two fiscal years, the City 
paid Cintas $116,378.40 (fiscal year 2021-22) and $111,908.80 (2020-21). 
Pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.070(h)(3), the City may use any purchasing procedures approved by 
the City Council if the City Council makes a finding concurrent with the award of the contract that the procedures are in 
the best interest of the City. Purchasing cooperative organizations such as OMNIA Partners offer competitively priced 
contracts for goods and services, avoids the administrative burden of completing a request for proposals process, and 
assures competitive prices are received. Based on the above, staff recommends approval of Change Order No. 2 in the 
amount of $20,000.01 to the City’s current contract with Cintas for a new not to exceed contract total of $120,000 for 
fiscal year 2022-23. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The Public Works and Parks & Recreation Departments are expected to be able to absorb the additional cost within their 
current budget authority. As such, no additional budget appropriations are needed.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This change order is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

CITY OF SAN MATEO

All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2023)
RESOLUTION APPROVING AN ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE APPROVING CHANGE ORDER NO. 2 
WITH CINTAS INC. FOR AN AMOUNT OF $20,000.01 FOR A TOTAL NOT TO EXCEED AMOUNT OF $120,000 FOR 
FISCAL YEAR 2022-23
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo has an existing contract with Cintas Inc. to provide uniform 
maintenance services for the Public Works and Parks and Recreation Department; and
WHEREAS, the existing agreement was based on a proposal by Cintas Inc. procured by OMNIA Partners; 
and 
WHEREAS, Omnia Partners is a cooperative purchasing organization specializing in public sector 
procurement; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.070(h)(3), the City may use any 
purchasing procedures approved by the City Council if the City Council makes a finding concurrent with the 
award of the contract that the procedures are in the best interest of the City; and
WHEREAS, purchasing cooperative organizations such as OMNIA Partners offer competitively priced 
contracts for goods and services, avoids the administrative burden of completing a request for proposal process, 
and assures competitive prices are received; and
WHEREAS, based on service use trends to date, the City is anticipated to pay Cintas Inc. approximately 
$120,000, which is $20,000.01 more than the annual contract authority of less than $100,000 for fiscal year 
2022-23; and
WHEREAS, the City requests contract authority from City Council in an amount not to exceed 
$120,000.00 for fiscal year 2022-23.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that: 
1.
This change order is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a governmental fiscal activity which 
does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant 
physical impact on the environment (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).) 
2.
Pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h), the approval of Change Order No. 2 is in 
the best interest of the City because:
a.
The City has an existing contract with Cintas Inc.;
b.
Cintas has provided uniform maintenance services for the City since fiscal year 2011-12;
c.
OMNIA Partners procurement services avoids costs normally associated with the administrative 
burden of completing a request for proposals process; and
d.
The OMNIA Partners procurement services assures a competitively priced contract for services.
108 of 536

--- page 109 ---
3.
Change Order No. 2 in the amount of $20,000.01 for a total not to exceed contract total of $120,000 
for fiscal year 2022-23 is approved.

. Adding Employees Additional employees and Merchandise may be added to this Agreement at any time upon written or oral request by
the Customer to the Company. Any such additional employees or Merchandise shall automatically become a part of and subject to the terms
of this Agreement. If such employees are employed at a Customer location that is then participating under this Agreement, the Customer
shall pay Company the one-time preparation fee indicated on Exhibit A. Customer shall not pay Company any one-time preparation fee for
garments for employces included in the initial installation of a Customer location. There will be a one-time charge for name and/or
company emblems when employees are added to the program in garments requiring emblems.

» Embiem Guarantee Customer has requested that Company supply emblems designed exclusively for Customer featuring Customer's logo or
other specific identification (hereinafter "Customer Emblems"). Company will maintain a sufficient quantity of Customer Emblems in
inventory to provide for Customer's needs and maintain a low cost per emblem through quantity purchases.

10. In the event Customer decides to discontinue the use of Customer Emblems, changes the design of the Customer Emblems, terminates this
Agreement for any reason or fails to renew this Agreement, the Customer will purchase at the time of deletion, design change, termination
or expiration, all remaining Customer Emblems that the Company allocated to Customer at the price indicated on Exhibit A of this
Agreement. In no event shall the number of Customer Emblems allocated to Customer exceed the greater of (a) twelve (12) months’
volume for each unique Customer Emblem or (b) a quantity agreed to by Company and Customer and noted on Exhibit A.

11. Terminating Employees Subject to the provisions of this Agreement, the weekly rental charge attributable to any individual leaving the
employ of the Customer, or on a temporary leave of absence of three (3) weeks or more, shall be terminated upon oral or written notice by
the Customer to the Company but only after all garments issued to that individual, or value of same at the then current Loss/Damage
Replacement Values, are returned to Company.

12. Replacement In the event any Merchandise is lost, stolen or is not returned to Company, or is destroyed or damaged by fire, welding
damage, acid, paint, ink, chemicals, neglect or otherwise, the Customer agrees to pay for said Merchandise at the then current Loss/Damage
Replacement Values.

13. Indemnification To the fullest extent permitted by law, Company agrees to defend, indemnify, pay on behalf of and save harmless the
Participating Public Agency, its elected and appointed officials, agents, employees and authorized volunteers against any and all claims,
liability, demands, suits or loss, including reasonable attorneys fees and all other costs connected therewith, arising out of or connected to
the services provided by Company under this Contract, but only to the extent of Company’s negligence.

14. Additional Items; Additional customer employees, products and services may be added to this agreement and shall automatically become a

part of and subject to the terms hereof and all of its provisions. If this agreement is terminated early for convenience, the parties agree that

the damages sustained by Company will be substantial and difficult to ascertain. Therefore, if this agreement is terminated by Customer
prior to the applicable expiration date for any reason other than documented quality of service reasons which are not cured, or terminated by

Company for non-payment by Customer at any time Customer will pay to Company, as termination charges and not as a penalty based upon

the following schedule:

If this agreement is cancelled for convenience in the first twelve months of the term, Customer shall pay as termination charges equal to 50
weeks of rental service.

If this agreement is cancelled for convenience in months thirteen (13) through eighteen (18) of the term, Customer shall pay as termination
charges equal to 36 weeks of rental service.

If this agreement is cancelled for convenience in months nineteen (19) through twenty-four (24) of the term, Customer shall pay as
termination charges equal to 23 weeks of rental service.

If this agreement is cancelled for convenience after 24 months of service, Customer shall pay as termination charges of 10 weeks of rental
service.

Customer shall also be responsible to return all of the Merchandise allocated to such Customer locations terminating this Agreement at the
then current Loss/Damage Replacement Values and for any unpaid charges on Customer’s account prior to termination,

City Manager's Office
MEETING DATE:
June 20, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Downtown Business Improvement Area – Annual Report 2022-23
RECOMMENDATION:
Accept the fiscal year 2022-23 Downtown San Mateo Association Annual Report.
BACKGROUND:
The Downtown Business Improvement Area (DBIA) was formed in 1986 for the purpose of improving Downtown San 
Mateo and promoting businesses within the area. The Downtown San Mateo Association (DSMA) has served as the DBIA 
administrator since its formation. Annually, the DSMA provides the City Council with an annual report of activities and 
expenditures for the previous fiscal year and proposes an assessment schedule for the upcoming fiscal year, as required by 
the California Streets and Highways Code Section 36533. 
On October 17, 2022, City Council approved DSMA's request for an assessment modification to change the basis and 
method of levy for the DBIA. Prior to this request, the DSMA has not had any revenue adjustments since 2010. 
Additionally, the DSMA wanted to change the basis and method of levy to better reflect the current composition of 
Downtown businesses from retail stores to a mix of office, medical and service users. At the subsequent meeting on 
November 7, 2022, the City Council held a public hearing on the modification of assessments and approved the new 
assessments. The updated assessment schedule starts July 1, 2023.  Typically, the DSMA brings forward their annual report 
and request to levy their assessment in June of every year. Since the annual public hearing for the levy is already approved, 
the DSMA is returning to City Council solely to provide their annual report describing their activities and plans for the next 
fiscal year. 
Since 2018, the DSMA has managed to continue its basic functions without a full-time Executive Director. This has been 
made possible due to dedicated all-volunteer board members and administrative support from the City Manager’s office. 
With the approval of the new DBIA assessment, the DSMA board members quickly worked on hiring a full-time Executive 
Director. After an exhaustive recruitment process, the new Executive Director will start on July 3, 2023, and will share 
office space with the Downtown Coordinator in the City-owned office at the Main Street Garage. The City will continue to 
provide the shared office space for at no cost to DSMA. 
One of the first duties of the DSMA Executive Director is to proactively reach out to Downtown businesses, initiate public 
community events in the Downtown, and promote the Downtown as a go-to destination on the Peninsula. With the role 
filled and the assessments modified, the DSMA believes the organization is finally able to provide the type of support 
Downtown businesses deserve, which is an organization that produces high-quality events, implements beautification 
projects, and actively promotes Downtown businesses. DSMA is excited to work with the City, Chamber, and community to 
make the Downtown a vibrant place for everyone. 
116 of 536

--- page 117 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
In May 2023, the DSMA submitted its annual report, incorporating a summary of fiscal year 2022-23 activities and 
proposed program and budget for fiscal year 2023-24 included as Attachment 1. The DSMA’s projected revenue for the 
next year is approximately $375,000 in estimated DBIA assessments. The budgeted expenditures are $375,000. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Acceptance of DSMA's annual report does not have an impact on the General Fund. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This report is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

--- page 118 ---
Downtown San Mateo Association 
Fiscal Year 2023 Annual Report 
July 1, 2022 - June 30, 2023 
  
Overview 
Since 1986, the DSMA has been designated by the City Council to manage the Downtown 
Business Improvement District. Originally activated to serve a largely retail-oriented district, 
the DSMA now manages a district with a much broader range of businesses such as tech 
startups, medical uses, restaurants, salons, gyms, bakeries, and a twelve-screen movie theater. 
 
With the departure of the executive director in 2018, the DSMA has operated through the 
efforts of an all-volunteer board and limited administrative assistance from City Hall. The 
DSMA partnership with the City Manager’s office over the past few years have produced a 
positive, strategic, and proactive relationship with the City of San Mateo. 
 
Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, businesses, employees, and residents have had their lives 
changed as government agencies mandated restrictions in order to slow the spread of the virus. 
Fiscal year 2023 (July 1, 2022- June 30, 2023) was the first year that all government restrictions 
ended. The low rate of COVID-19 infections, increased number of vaccinated residents, and 
elimination of government mandates, have created a bit more normalcy as people and 
businesses return to their pre-COVID routines and operations. 
 
While revenues have not fully returned to pre-pandemic times, the DSMA remain optimistic 
about the continued economic recovery in the Downtown. As companies return to the office 
with hybrid work schedules, there is a corresponding increase in customers supporting coffee 
shops, salons, bakeries, gyms, dry cleaners, and other retailers in the Downtown. Additionally, 
San Mateo remain a premier destination location for Bay Area foodies as the Downtown bustles 
with visitors and residents during meal hours.  
 
The City’s support of outdoor dining has helped many businesses remain open through the 
pandemic. Even though the City’s temporary outdoor dining program has ended, businesses 
have been able to apply and receive permanent parklets and sidewalk dining permits. As the 
118 of 536

--- page 119 ---
summer approaches, DSMA is hopeful for more businesses to apply and receive outdoor dining 
permits.  
 
While the overall economic conditions in the Downtown remain positive, the post COVID-19 
environment still has a lingering effect on the local economy, the downtown, and the country. 
The DSMA will continue its efforts to help small businesses thrive, grow, and remain resilient 
despite economic headwinds such as rising interest rates heading into FY24.  
 
In FY23, the DSMA accomplished the following: 
● Successfully worked with the DSMA members and San Mateo City Council to 
approve a rate modifications to the Downtown Business Improvement Area’s rate 
structure for long-term sustainability for the district. 
● The DSMA began its search for an Executive Director to bring focused leadership and 
expanded activity for our Downtown constituents. 
● Monitored restaurant and business changes in Downtown San Mateo and updated 
information on the DSMA website, social media, and print ads. 
● Promoted local businesses with weekly ads in the local newspaper to encourage public 
support for our small businesses with take out, curbside pickup, and online shopping 
experiences. 
● Continuously worked with the City Manager’s office to collaborate on DSMA efforts. 
● Continued a strong partnership with the Public Works, Downtown Coordinator to 
identify Downtown issues and coordinate solutions. 
● Encouraged the review of the proposed issue of the Downtown public bathroom. 
● Held regular monthly board meetings (both live and online), keeping a large number of 
board members engaged throughout the pandemic. 
● Continued a close working relationship with City Council members. 
● Produced two monthly e-newsletters (one to BID members and one to the community 
at large) announcing Downtown news. 
● Contracted the Downtown Cleaning Porter, seven days a week, to keep our Downtown 
clean, sanitary and welcoming for customers and visitors. 
● Enforced strict financial restraints on the annual budget to both provide services and 
retain as much savings as possible. 
● Responded to dozens of emails and phone messages from both members of the 
community and business owners.   
 
During the last year, several new businesses opened in the Downtown, some examples are 
Kajiken Ramen, Somi Somi, and Indigo Rugs. Additionally, Curry Pizza, Zhangliang Malatang 
Hot Pot, Paris Baguette and many more will be opening in later 2023. This demonstrates the 
continued strength of Downtown San Mateo as a location for small business investment.  
119 of 536

--- page 120 ---
Finally, the DSMA would like to recognize Downtown businesses for keeping businesses going 
and providing crucial amenities and services to the Downtown. The DSMA also appreciates the 
efforts of the City of San Mateo to help keep our Downtown businesses healthy and vibrant 
with the creation of the Pedestrian Mall between 1st and 3rd Avenues along B Street, permanent 
outdoor dining permits, construction of the new affordable housing and public garage at KiKu 
Crossing, and their continued support of the DSMA. 
Downtown San Mateo 
 
Thanks to its regionally central location and proximity to CalTrain, Downtown San Mateo is an 
employment hub and a popular place to meet for both business and leisure purposes. 
Commuters and leisure visitors come from San Francisco, Santa Clara County and the East Bay, 
as well as nearby Peninsula communities.  Downtown is known for its vast abundance and 
variety of restaurants, which comprise more than 20% of district businesses. It is also an 
important role in the Silicon Valley ecosystem, hosting the offices of start-ups and tech-based 
companies large and small.  The presence of Mills Medical Center means that Downtown also 
includes many medical professionals and other healthcare uses.  While there are traditional 
retailers in the district, the storefronts on most blocks are dominated by food purveyors, along 
with services such as salons, fitness studios, and banks.  The blocks east of the railroad tracks 
also have a variety of automotive uses and is experiencing a building boom of mixed residential 
and professional buildings.  The DSMA will look to incorporate the businesses that will tenant 
the new construction surrounding the Downtown at future board meetings. 
  
While the Downtown hoped for a quicker return to work for daytime employees, the number of 
visitors and workers remain less than it was before the pandemic.  This is due to the shift of 
employees working in technology, financial, and professional industries to work from home for 
2-5 days a week.  It is unknown if this is a permanent change in the foot traffic or if the same 
number of people still come to the Downtown, but on fewer days. However, the evening 
restaurant businesses are seeing a closer pre-pandemic return to normal in revenues. 
 
Visitors have complimented the DSMA on the high-level of cleanliness in the Downtown area. 
Thus, it is important to continue maintaining a clean Downtown to attract visitors and local 
residents. The DSMA has addressed cleanliness by contracting with Universal Site Services for 
a porter. Under the current contract, the DSMA contracts a cleaning porter to wipe trash cans 
and newspaper racks, sweep sidewalks and gutters, and ensure Downtown is clean and 
attractive throughout the week. DSMA’s cleaning efforts increases the frequency, duration, and 
enhances the cleaning services provided by the City.   
120 of 536

--- page 121 ---
The DSMA utilizes social media to promote Downtown’s unique offerings and inform both 
businesses and the public of news that affects them in a very timely fashion.  The DSMA’s 
social media efforts have proven to be an extremely effective way to attract Downtown visitors, 
particularly appreciated by restaurants in the Downtown.  Social media outreach also produces 
monthly newsletters (one oriented to the public and the other for Downtown businesses) and 
provides updates on the social media platforms of interesting current affairs. 
  
In November 2022, DSMA accomplished a huge long-term goal to change the existing 
assessment modification to better serve the current mix of businesses in the Downtown. The 
change in rates will be effective as of July 1, 2023. The reasons for the assessment change was 
that the DSMA had not seen an assessment increase in thirteen years. The change in revenues 
will help the DSMA attract and hire an Executive Director who can increase member outreach, 
communication, create more public community events, and help market the Downtown as a go-
to destination on the Peninsula.  The DSMA maintains that dedicating the assessment funds for 
the Downtown is necessary to deliver high-quality customer experience, implement 
beautification initiatives, promote Downtown businesses, and provide greater equity among the 
mix of Downtown businesses in the future.  
 
While there is always more that can be done to support and promote Downtown businesses, 
DSMA is proud of the work the all-volunteer board have done. We wish to continue our work 
to help Downtown businesses, continue to collaborate with the City to support the Downtown 
and requests Council’s continue support of the organization.  
DSMA FOCUS AREAS 
DSMA activities for the past several years have addressed three focus areas:  
● Civic Partnership   
● Community Engagement   
● BID Member Engagement  
Civic Partnerships 
The DSMA routinely works with city staff from Public Works, Planning, Parks and 
Recreation, and the City Manager’s office on projects affecting Downtown.  Here is a 
summary from the past year. 
121 of 536

--- page 122 ---
Downtown Beautification and Cleanliness 
● Provide daily oversight of private contractor for cleaning porter services. 
● Contracts a private contractor to install seasonal banners on light posts. 
● Cooperates with Downtown Post Office to ensure landscaping and sidewalks are kept 
clean. 
● Work with City to monitor trash ordinance in the Downtown. 
Additional civic partnership activities  
● Work closely with the San Mateo Police Department on public safety downtown; the 
SMPD is an active participant in the monthly DSMA Board of Directors meetings.  
● Business outreach for city initiatives for outdoor dining, pedestrian mall, public art and 
similar. 
● Outreach and attendance at neighborhood association meetings. 
Community Engagement  
Public outreach builds interest among local audiences and invites them to explore Downtown 
San Mateo for a variety of experiences.  As we emerge from the pandemic community 
engagement events will be reengaged.  The DSMA looks forward to partnering with the City, 
Chamber of Commerce, and other non-profits to promote community special events in the 
Downtown.  
Marketing Efforts  
Print Materials 
● Created and circulated a listing of businesses and restaurants that are operating during the 
last year in local newspaper, the San Mateo Daily Journal continuously over the holiday 
season.  
● Continuously updated restaurant and retail store information in successive on our website 
keep the public updated on store hours and availability. 
Social Media 
● Enables DSMA to share news about our businesses and extend the marketing reach of 
individual businesses.  Our combined Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram audiences now 
exceed 13,000 followers. 
● Downtown San Mateo Monthly Update email blast reaches about 1,250 community 
subscribers and business owners.

● Improved Media Content will include features of local restaurants and businesses,

Website   
• The website provides greater information, search capabilities, and dedicated pages to 
specific information for the public and Downtown businesses to access. 
• Created a data-rich, accessible, and easily maintained website that will service as another 
strong avenue for Downtown San Mateo outreach.  
• The DSMA will work with the newly hired Executive Director to market the website and 
enhance as a tool for the public to explore and become informed about the Downtown. 
 
 
BID Member Engagement Milestones FY23 
  
While BID membership is automatic based on a district address, awareness and participation is 
always trying to be improved. The DSMA strives to engage BID members and add value to the 
varied businesses located Downtown.  Examples of engagement by the DSMA include:  
  
● Personal outreach visits by DSMA board members to new Downtown businesses. 
● Coordination of regular outdoor cleaning services to promote a Downtown in which 
people enjoy spending time. 
● Regular features of new Downtown businesses via social media. 
● Held regular DSMA Board meetings, inviting new and existing businesses to attend, and 
the City to present topical presentations to the Board. 
● Increased consistency of board member attendance and participation. 
 
Additional DSMA Milestones FY23 
 
● Strengthen economic development collaboration with local organizations as well as the 
City of San Mateo. 
DSMA Goals for FY24 
 
123 of 536

--- page 124 ---
While businesses are returning to normal, the new fiscal year will be filled with some 
unpredictability due to the challenges of post pandemic changes and the economy fluctuations. 
Consequently, DSMA’s role as an advocate, facilitator, and organizer of Downtown businesses 
will be mission critical in the post COVID economy. 
 
DSMA will continue to help the City provide a high-level of customer service with enhanced 
cleaning services; attract visitors to shop and eat in Downtown businesses with promotional 
efforts; and convene Downtown business owners and property owners to support each other 
through the crises while helping the City understand the needs of the Downtown businesses.   
 
Most of all, DSMA will help Downtown businesses by being a resource to businesses, a partner 
with the City of San Mateo, and an organization dedicated to keep the Downtown an attractive 
place for visitors. 
 
● Continue to support marketing, outreach, and public safety information. 
● Strengthen economic development collaborations with local organizations. 
● Develop a communications plan to further the DSMA brand.

● Explore the possibility of college interns to help with DSMA operations and events. 
● Integrate the newly hired Executive Director into the fabric of Downtown as quickly and 
smoothly as possible. 
● Research the impact of installing a public restroom Downtown closer to the core of the 
Downtown. 
 
In summary, the DSMA seeks the continued support of the City Council.  The DSMA works to 
attract visitors, promote local businesses, and maintain a clean Downtown.   
 
Budget 2024 (FY24) Overview 
 
The DSMA has worked through this pandemic by serving its constituents.  The DSMA has 
run at deficit spending for a couple years to help our members.  The City Council restored 
member fee to 100% in FY23 to help restore the long-term viability of the DSMA. 
 
The DSMA FY24 Budget has a planned balanced budget.  This neutral budget is a reflection 
of the commitment of the DSMA to provide support and services to the Downtown area while 
staying within its budgetary parameters.  Planned expenses for FY24 are thoughtfully planned 
to continue services and encourage patrons for the Downtown businesses.  The following are a 
summary of the major budget highlights (detailed the Budget Comparative Overview 
Appendix): 
• Revenue projections are increasing due to the expectation of business returning to a 
more normal environment in the FY24 and the future impact of the rate modification.  
The exact revenue goal is an estimate as it will be based on the businesses mix. 
• Expenses will increase in the FY24 Budget due primarily to the budgeted expense of a 
full time Executive Director (ED).  The expense highlights are: 
o Program Activities 
§ Supporting most of the ED payroll 
§ Public events 
§ Internship program  
o Civic Partnership 
§ Downtown Cleaning Porter 
§ Partial ED salary 
o Marketing services  
§ Advertising and Promotion 
§ Social Media consultant 
§ Partial ED salary 
125 of 536

--- page 126 ---
o Administration 
o A small contingency fund for unplanned expenses at these uncertain times 
 
The DSMA projects a cash balance of $173.0K at the start of FY24 and projects a cash 
balance of approximately $173.0K at the end of the budgeted FY24.

City Council

PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
June 20, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Worker Resource Center Operations – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Samaritan House for operations of the Worker Resource Center for the period of from July 1, 
2023 through June 30, 2025 in the amount of $195,000 per year and authorize City Manager to execute the agreement in 
substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
In June 2003, the City Council approved an agreement with Samaritan House to undertake the operation of the Worker 
Resource Center (WRC) located at the parking lot on Fifth Avenue at the railroad tracks. The center was created to 
alleviate the impact of day workers congregating along the Third and Fourth Avenue corridors in Downtown. These 
corridors were previously characterized by a variety of roadway violations jeopardizing traffic and pedestrian safety, and 
also resulted in police service calls related to loitering, urinating in public, disturbances, and trespassing complaints. The 
main purpose of the WRC is to match job seekers with job providers in a safe location that does not interfere with traffic 
and pedestrian flows. This working relationship has continued since inception through the existing agreement ending June 
30, 2023.
Services Provided Under the Agreement
The Center’s operations include a place for workers and seekers of casual labor to meet every day except for holidays. As 
workers come to the center, they register with center staff and identify their home address and work skills, such as manual 
labor, gardening, painting, or carpentry. Residents and businesses seeking casual labor coming to the center are asked 
what type of skill they are seeking and are matched with workers. Samaritan House also provides or refers other services 
as needed to the workers (health, dental, nutrition, and clothing) either at the center or other locations.
Center Location
The Worker Resource Center has a lease in place for its new location at 171 N. Amphlett Boulevard, and Samaritan House 
will continue to operate the center from this space for the foreseeable future.
BUDGET IMPACT:
This is a two-year agreement in the amount of $195,000 per year, which is budgeted in the General Fund. This does not 
completely cover the costs of the operations for Samaritan House, as they also provide about $70,000 from their own fund 
raising to ensure the WRC services are provided. About 80% of the program budget is for staffing consisting of three full-
time equivalent employees.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
129 of 536

CITY OF SAN MATEO
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
