AGREEMENT TO PROVIDE SERVICES TO OPERATE 
 THE WORKER RESOURCE CENTER 
 
 
 
This agreement (“Agreement”) is made and entered into this ____ ________________ by 
and between the SAMARITAN HOUSE (“ORGANIZATION”), a nonprofit corporation, and the 
City of San Mateo (“CITY”), a municipal corporation.  
 
 
The CITY wishes to provide employment, social, educational and other services promoting 
public health and safety for workers at the Worker Resource Center. 
 
 
The ORGANIZATION is willing to and capable of performing such community services. 
 
 
To achieve common goals in the community, the CITY and the ORGANIZATION agree as 
follows: 
 
I. 
CITY AND ORGANIZATION RESPONSIBILITIES: 
 
 
A. CITY will provide the site for a Workers Resource Center to provide employment, 
social and educational services and other services promoting public health and safety. 
 
B. CITY has secured a lease for space for the Workers Resource Center located at 171 
N. Amphlett Blvd, San Mateo, CA (see Attachment A “Lease”) and agrees to pay for the 
base rent, common area operating expenses, and utilities.  The owner of the property is 
Sakai Family Partnership (“Lessor”) and has designated SC Properties as its agent and 
property manager (“Property Manager”). 
 
C. ORGANIZATION shall not violate any provisions of the Lease 
 
D. ORGANIZATION will provide and may partner with other social and educational 
agencies to provide, services, including but not limited to educational services, health, 
nutrition, medical, basic skills classes. 
 
E. CITY will fund the Workers Resource Center.  ORGANIZATION may assist CITY 
in seeking and securing additional funding for center operations, but ORGANIZATION 
is under no obligation to do so.  
 
F. ORGANIZATION is responsible for the safety, security and operations of the 
facility, including the performance of all routine custodial and maintenance duties, 
maintenance of consumable supplies, and litter control at the facility.  
 
G. ORGANIZATION shall contact Property Manger for repairs that are 
the responsibility of the Lessor as described in paragraph 7.2 of the Lease. 
ORGANIZATION shall copy CITY on any correspondence with Property 
Manager. 
 
 
131 of 536

--- page 132 ---
Page 2 of 6 
 
H. ORGANIZATION shall perform all services under the Agreement in accordance 
with all applicable Federal, State, County and municipal laws, ordinances, regulations, 
and funding mandates, including requirements pertaining to confidentiality, civil rights, 
and quality assurance.  
 
I. 
 ORGANIZATION will timely and accurately complete, sign, and submit all 
necessary documentation of compliance 
 
J. 
ORGANIZATION will adhere to the Operational Goals as identified in Attachment 
B– Operational Goals. 
 
II. 
AGREEMENT TERM:  The services to be performed under this Agreement will 
commence on July 1, 2023, and will continue until June 30, 2025, unless terminated earlier by 
either party in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.   
 
III. 
FUNDING:  Funding by CITY to ORGANIZATION shall be on the following terms: 
 
A. Funding for FY2023-24 and FY 2024-25 shall not exceed $195,000 annually as 
defined in Attachment C-Worker Resource Center Budget. ORGANIZATION shall 
provide an invoice for each request for payment on a monthly basis based on hours 
worked and overhead incurred in accordance with the expenses described in the budget in 
Attachment C.  CITY will pay the invoice within 30 days of receipt.    
 
 
IV. 
FISCAL RESPONSIBILITIES OF ORGANIZATION:  ORGANIZATION shall: 
 
A. Establish and maintain a written system of accounts for budgeted funds that 
conforms with generally accepted accounting principles.  Such system of accounts shall 
be subject to review, approval and inspection of CITY. 
 
B. Document all costs by maintaining complete and accurate records of all financial 
transactions, including, but not limited to, contracts, invoices, time cards, cash and charge 
receipts, vouchers, cancelled checks, bank statements and/or other official documentation 
evidencing in proper detail the nature and propriety of all charges. 
 
C. If ORGANIZATION has an independent fiscal audit or other annual financial status 
report compiled, a copy shall be provided to the CITY within 30 days of completion and 
acceptance by the ORGANIZATION Board of Directors. 
 
D. ORGANIZATION is liable for repayment of disallowed costs.  Disallowed costs 
may be identified through audits, examination of invoices, monitoring or other sources.  
ORGANIZATION shall be afforded the opportunity to respond to any adverse findings 
which may lead to disallowed costs.  Disallowed costs are costs not allowed and 
approved by CITY.  Disallowed costs are generally defined as funds expended for 
purposes which do not directly further the common goals of ORGANIZATION, and 
CITY, set forth in the second paragraph on page 1 of this Agreement. 
 
132 of 536

--- page 133 ---
Page 3 of 6 
 
V. 
RECORDS, REPORTS AND AUDITS OF ORGANIZATION: 
 
A. ORGANIZATION shall maintain records, including but not limited to books, 
financial records, supporting documents, statistical records, personnel, property, 
performance measures as identified in Attachment D – Performance Measures, and all 
other pertinent records sufficient to reflect properly (1) all direct and indirect costs of 
whatever nature claimed to have been incurred and anticipated to be incurred to perform 
this Agreement, and (2) all other matters covered by this Agreement. 
 
B. Preservation of Records:  ORGANIZATION shall preserve and make available its 
records related to this Agreement for the following periods: 
 
1.Until the expiration of three (3) years from the date of the submission of the 
annual financial status report; 
2.For such longer period, if any, as is required by applicable law; or 
3.If this Agreement is completely or partially terminated, the records relating to 
the work terminated shall be preserved and made available for a period of three 
(3) years from the date of any resulting final settlement. 
 
VI. TERMINATION:   
 
A. In the event funds are not available from CITY or other third-party entities, CITY 
may terminate the Agreement upon 90 days written notice and is under no obligation to 
continue to provide services as identified in Section 1.  If the Agreement is terminated in 
this manner, ORGANIZATION shall be reimbursed for all valid expenditures made up to 
and including the effective date of the termination. 
 
B. Notwithstanding availability of funding, the CITY or ORGANIZATION may 
terminate this Agreement with 90 days’ notice if CITY or ORGANIZATION deems it to 
be in its best interest to discontinue the program.  If the Agreement is terminated in this 
manner, CITY agrees to reimburse ORGANIZATION for all valid expenditures made up 
to and including the effective date of the termination.  
 
VII. 
INTEREST OF PUBLIC OFFICIALS:   No members, officers, employees or agents of 
the CITY, shall have any interest, direct or indirect, in this Agreement or a related subcontract. 
 
VIII. 
INSPECTION OF PROGRAM:   It is understood that CITY will periodically review 
ORGANIZATION's program. 
 
IX. 
PROGRAM INCOME:   ORGANIZATION shall record program income as part of the 
financial transactions of the program.  Any income generated from the program may be used to 
offset expenses and shall not be considered as funding stipulated in Article 3 in this Agreement.   
 
X. 
REVERSION OF ASSETS:   If the ORGANIZATION is dissolved or ceases to exist, 
any unexpended program funds in the possession of ORGANIZATION at the time of dissolution 
133 of 536

--- page 134 ---
Page 4 of 6 
 
shall be immediately returned to CITY.   
 
XI. 
ASSIGNABILITY:   ORGANIZATION shall not assign this Agreement and shall not 
transfer any interest in this Agreement without the prior written consent of CITY. 
 
XII. 
INDEMNIFICATION: 
 
A. Each party shall indemnify and hold harmless the other party and its agents and 
employees from and against all claims, damages, losses and expenses, including but not 
limited to attorney’s fees, arising out of or resulting from its performance of its 
obligations set forth in this Agreement, excepting claims, damages, losses and expenses 
resulting from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the other party for which that 
party will be responsible. 
 
B. CITY shall further indemnify ORGANIZATION from such claims, damages, losses 
and expenses arising out of the use or occupancy of the premises by any guests or 
invitees, except to the extent that any such claim, damage, loss, or expense is caused by 
ORGANIZATION’s failure to operate and maintain the premises in a safe condition 
during the hours of operation of the center. 
 
C. The intent of this section is to provide that if someone is injured on the premises 
during its hours of operation because of a condition caused by ORGANIZATION, its 
agents or employees, or a condition that ORGANIZATION or its agents or employees in 
exercising reasonable efforts could have addressed, the ORGANIZATION shall bear the 
loss.  If someone is injured on the premises at any time because of faulty construction or 
improper maintenance, then the CITY shall bear the risk of premises liability. 
 
D. For purposes of the Agreement, “claims” means any and all claims, losses, costs, 
damages, expenses, liabilities, liens, actions, causes of action (whether in tort or contract, 
law or equity or otherwise), charges, assessments, fines, and penalties of any kind 
(including consultant and expert expenses, court costs, and attorney fees actually 
incurred). 
 
E. The clauses of this Article 12 shall survive the expiration or earlier termination of 
this Agreement until all claims against the parties hereto involving any of the indemnified 
matters are fully, finally, and absolutely barred by the applicable statute of limitations. 
 
XIII. INSURANCE:  ORGANIZATION shall provide and maintain: 
 
A. Commercial General Liability Insurance, occurrence form, at least as broad as 
Insurance Services Office (ISO) form CG 0001, with a limit of not less than $1,000,000 
each occurrence and a $3,000,000 umbrella policy.  If such insurance contains a general 
aggregate limit, it shall apply separately to this Agreement or be no less than two (2) 
times the occurrence limit. 
 
B. Automobile Liability Insurance, at least as broad as ISO Form CA 0001, with a limit 
of not less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.  Such 
134 of 536

Page 5 of 6 
 
insurance shall include coverage for owned, hired, and non-owned automobiles. 
 
C. Workers Compensation in at least the minimum statutory limits, with Employer’s 
Liability Insurance limits of at least $1,000,000 per accident. 
 
D. General Provisions for all insurance.  The general and auto liability insurance 
policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following: 
1.  Include the City of San Mateo, its elected and appointed officials, employees, 
volunteers and agents as insureds with respect to this Agreement and the 
performance of services in this Agreement (does not apply to workers compensation 
or auto insurance).  The coverage shall contain no special limitations on the scope of 
its protection to the above-designated insureds. 
2.  Be primary with respect to any insurance or self-insurance programs of City, its 
elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. 
3.  A waiver of all rights of subrogation against the City of San Mateo, its elected 
and appointed officials, employees, volunteers and agents for losses paid under the 
terms of any policy which arise from the work performed by the CONSULTANT.  
This provision also applies to the Workers’ Compensation policy. 
4.  Be evidenced, prior to commencement of services, by properly executed policy 
endorsements in addition to a certificate of insurance. 
5.  No changes in insurance may be made without the written approval of the City 
Attorney's Office. 
 
XIV. 
NONDISCRIMINATION: 
 
A. No person shall, on the grounds of race, color, national origin, religious affiliation or 
non-affiliation, marital status, medical condition, sex, age, disability status, sexual 
orientation or political affiliation, be excluded from participation in, be denied the 
benefits, or be subjected to discrimination or harassment under this Agreement. 
 
B. ORGANIZATION shall ensure equal employment opportunity based on objective 
standards of recruitment, selection, promotion, classification, compensation, performance 
evaluations, and management relations, for all employees under this Agreement.  
ORGANIZATION's personnel policies shall be made available to CITY upon request.

ATTACHMENT B 
 
WORKER RESOURCE CENTER 
OPERATIONAL GOALS 
 
 
 
 
 
• Center will be open during peak periods of casual labor work solicitation, generally early 
mornings to mid-day up to seven days a week 
• Center operations will be scaleable to needs 
• ORGANIZATION will perform intake and assessment of both workers and employers  
• ORGANIZATION will use an equitable system to enable workers to match with job 
providers 
• ORGANIZATION will integrate other services onsite with a consortium of providers: ESL 
classes, clothing, nutrition, health screening, etc.  
• ORGANIZATION will only provide services and resource referrals to registered WRC 
workers 
• ORGANIZATION will inform potential employers of their obligation to insure workers for 
on-the-job injuries. 
• ORGANIZATION will use volunteers  
• CITY and ORGANIZATION will perform outreach and marketing of center to laborers and 
job providers at least twice a month 
• ORGANIZATION will abide by Center rules and regulations 
• ORGANIZATION will involve workers in developing rules and regulations 
• ORGANIZATION will involve workers in facility maintenance 
• ORGANIZATION will prepare Data collection/monthly reports on use of center to measure 
center effectiveness 
• ORGANIZATION will Regularly liaison with CITY 
• ORGANIZATION will coordinate with CITY to secure third party funding for programs and 
activities.

ATTACHMENT D 
 
WORKER RESOURCE CENTER 
MEASURES OF SUCCESS 
 
 
 
Reported Quarterly:   
 
• Daily number of workers (clients) utilizing Worker Resource Center (WRC)  
• Number of unduplicated workers each month 
• Number of workers utilizing additional services each month 
• Daily number of jobs available at the WRC 
• Placement ratio of workers to jobs available 
• Number of job seekers daily on the streets (data provided by City of San Mateo Police 
Department) 
• Police calls for service in the immediate area surrounding the Worker Resource Center 
• Workers express satisfaction with WRC measured by monthly survey 
• Job providers express satisfaction with WRC measured by survey provided to every job 
provider. 
• Description of workers involvement in operations of WRC and community service 
activities 
• Description of types of program/services provided at WRC 
• Description of outreach efforts to workers and job providers 
• Efforts to obtain third party funding or volunteer services at the WRC 
 
 Reported Annually: 
 
• Ensure budget is adhered to 
• Local residents/owners, businesses and other stakeholders express satisfaction with 
impacts of WRC (measured by community survey)

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  19
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 23-3133
File ID: 23-7613
  TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
June 20, 2023 
SUBJECT: 
Community Development Block Grant Samaritan House – Appropriation
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to authorize the appropriation of funds in the amount of $56,031.02 from the Community 
Development Block Grant Fund to the Community Development Block Grant Community Facilities project to complete a 
contract with Samaritan House for public facilities upgrades.
BACKGROUND:
The Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Program provides flexible funding for community development projects 
and programs to assist individuals and families from extremely low- to moderate-income households as defined by the 
Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD). The City receives an annual entitlement grant based on a national 
formula and has participated in this program since 1977. City Council adopted the fiscal year 2022-23 CDBG Annual Plan 
on May 2, 2022.
Since 1974, Samaritan House has been a vital service provider in the community, addressing inequity for the underserved, 
low-income residents of San Mateo County through increased access to shelter and housing assistance; medical and 
dental clinics; clothes for children; personalized case management; and much more. The City approved Samaritan House’s 
request of $100,000 for public facilities upgrades in the 2022-23 CDBG Annual Plan. Identifying funding sources and 
selection of contractors have delayed construction, which is now expected to take place throughout the 2023-24 program 
year. To allow completion of the project, the contract will terminate on June 30, 2024.
The City of San Mateo maintains capital improvement project for public facilities improvements which use the CDBG 
funding, which the Samaritan House contract would draw from. At the beginning of fiscal year 2022-23, there was 
$43,968.98 of remaining CDBG funds in this project. The adopted 2022-23 budget outlined $100,000 for public facilities 
improvements in the CDBG program based upon the annual award from HUD. Therefore, $56,013.02 of CDBG funds must 
be appropriated into the project for the upcoming program year budget, which will increase the balance to $100,000.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The Community Development Block Grant Community Facilities Project (310520) budget currently has a remaining balance of 
approximately $43,969. Adoption of the resolution will appropriate an additional amount of $56,031 from the CDBG fund 
balance to this project, which will increase the remaining project budget to $100,000. With the approval of the 
appropriation, sufficient funding will be available to complete the Samaritan House contract. 
The CDBG grant funding operates on a reimbursement basis. All CDBG expenditures are reimbursed by HUD on a quarterly 
basis, therefore there is no impact to the General Fund.
161 of 536

--- page 162 ---
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This budget appropriation is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not 
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
RESOLUTION NO.  
 (2023)
APPROPRIATE $56,031 FROM THE COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT BLOCK GRANT FUND BALANCE TO 
THE CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROJECT 310520 (COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT BLOCK GRANT 
COMMUNITY FACILITIES)
WHEREAS, the City received $770,897 in federal funding from the U.S. Department of Housing and 
Urban Development for the Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) program for the 2022-2023 annual 
grant; and
WHEREAS, the City Council adopted the fiscal year 2022-23 CDBG Annual Plan which approved 
$100,000 for Samaritan House facility upgrades on May 2, 2022; and
WHEREAS, the Samaritan House renovation contract would draw from Capital Improvement Project 
310520 (Community Development Block Grant Community Facilities), which had a balance of $43,969 of 
remaining at the beginning of FY 2022-2023; and
WHEREAS, construction for the project has been delayed and is now expected to take place 
throughout the 2023-2024 program year; and
WHEREAS, a budget appropriation of $56,031 from the CDBG fund to Capital Improvement Project 310520 
(Community Development Block Grant Community Facilities) is required to address the deficit; and
WHEREAS, Section 5.13(a) of the City Charter requires City Council approval by resolution for 
supplemental appropriations to the City budget.
that:
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES
1 .  This budget appropriation is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal 
activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a 
potentially significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
2 .  An appropriation of funds in the amount of $56,031 from the Community Development Block Grant 
Fund restricted fund balance to the Capital Improvement Project 310520 (Community Development Block 
Grant Community Facilities) for a total budget of $100,000 is approved.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  20
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 26-3111
File ID: 23-7647
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
June 20, 2023 
SUBJECT:
On-Call Professional Planning Services - Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Good City Company for on-call professional technical planning services for a period of five 
years from July 1, 2023 through June 30, 2028 in the amount not to exceed $750,000 and authorize the Community 
Development Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The Community Development Department (CDD) frequently utilizes consultants to provide various specialized professional 
services to support the processing of planning and building applications. Given the volume of planning applications that 
CDD receives and processes, staff works with consultants on an as-needed basis, based on the complexity and specific 
needs of an application. To expand the group of consultants available to support the planning application review process, 
CDD issued a request for qualifications (RFQ) in fall of 2022 to identify firms interested in and qualified to provide various 
specialized planning support services on an on-call basis. 
Good City Company submitted a statement of qualifications that reflected their knowledge, technical expertise and broad 
experience in various aspects of Planning that the City anticipates in the coming years. The City also has an existing 
contract with Good City Company for Planning review and technical support services, which expires on June 30, 2023. To 
continue their technical support on the Housing Element and General Plan Update, and with other planning review 
services, staff is recommending that the City continue to use Good City Company to support Community Development 
Department operations. The new agreement would be for a five-year term, ending on June 30, 2028, and have a not-to-
exceed amount of $750,000. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The services provided by Good City Company would either be pass-through funds relating to private development 
applications and paid for by the applicant or would be accounted for in the Department budget associated with City 
initiatives or advanced planning projects. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

AGREEMENT WITH GOOD CITY COMPANY
FOR PROFESSIONAL CONSULTANT SERVICES
FOR 
On-Call Professional Planning Services 
This Agreement, made and entered into this day of _______________, by and between the 
CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and Good City Company (Good City), a California Corporation (“CONSULTANT”), whose 
address is 1351 Laurel St, San Carlos, CA 94070.
RECITALS:
A.  CITY desires certain on-call professional and technical Planning services hereinafter 
described.
B.  CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these on-call professional and 
technical Planning services by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services 
and CONSULTANT has offered to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set 
forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
166 of 536

--- page 167 ---
Professional Services
Page 2 of 11
Rev 9/22
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence on July 1, 2023 and be 
completed on June 30, 2028.
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee not to exceed 
$750,000, pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by 
reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing

Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.

origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
Perform on-call professional technical planning services, including:
1.
Specialized Planning Services: Site planning, project management (of long-planning range planning 
processes and development applications), staff report writing, design review, building permit 
review, Planning Commission facilitation, and other specialized project assistance.
2.
Policy Planning: Assist with creation and facilitation of policy planning initiatives such as General 
Plans, Specific Plans, Master Plans, Downtown/Transit Corridor Plans, Transportation Demand 
Management Strategies, and Zoning Codes.
3.
Housing Strategy: Assist in developing strategies to produce more housing for all income levels and 
implement Housing Element programs and policies.
4.
Policy Implementation: Implement and track Long-Range Planning documents, such as General 
Plans, Housing Elements, Downtown Plan, and Specific Plans.
5.
Permit Streamlining & Development Review: Review and make recommendations related to 
optimizing the development and project review processes, that meet the needs of the City and 
applicant, and follow State legislation and other regulatory agencies.
6.
Transportation Demand Management: Implement transportation-related strategy, policy planning, 
and monitoring services for development projects as part of the entitlement review and condition 
compliance.
7.
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA): Complete, facilitate, or manage consultants to 
complete initial reviews, categorical exemptions, negative declarations, and monitor projects 
consistent with CEQA.
8.
Outreach Support: Facilitate community meetings or open houses - whether they relate to a specific 
development project, policy planning topic, or broader community topics.

MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:

covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate 
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or 
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.

June 20, 2023 
SUBJECT: 
Sewer Service Charges for Fiscal Years 2023-24 to 2027-28 – Ordinance Adoption 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt an Ordinance to establish maximum sewer rates effective August 1, 2023 through June 30, 2028 pursuant to San 
Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.54.030 “Sewer Service Charges.” 
BACKGROUND:
On June 5, 2023, following a determination that a majority protest does not exist, the City Council introduced the attached 
proposed ordinance (Attachment 1) to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.54.030 “Sewer Service Charges” 
establishing maximum sewer rates effective August 1, 2023 through June 30, 2028.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
The proposed rate increases for the fiscal years 2023-24 to 2027-28 are calculated based on the projected revenue 
requirements to provide sewer service, and cover the City’s costs, including debt service, necessary reserves, and future 
capital expenditures. The proposed rate schedule sets the sewer service charge rates to increase a maximum of 3% each 
year. The actual revenue to be generated will not be known until the rate is set and complete water data is available and 
analyzed.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
In accordance with CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4), the adoption of this ordinance is not a project subject to CEQA, 
because the sewer service charge is a funding mechanism for the City Sewer operations and capital projects.
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.  
ATTACHMENTS

CITY OF SAN MATEO
DRAFT ORDINANCE
ESTABLISHING SEWER SERVICE CHARGES FOR FISCAL YEARS 2023-2024 THROUGH 2027-2028
WHEREAS, the procedures set forth in California Constitution Article XIII D, Section 6 for property-
related fees, enacted by Proposition 218, apply to sewer service charge increases; and
WHEREAS, a sewer service charge is a property-related fee for each parcel’s attributable use of the 
sanitary sewer collection and treatment systems in the City (“Sewer Service Charge”); and
WHEREAS, the City is following the procedures set forth in Article XIII D, Section 6 for the proposed 
Sewer Service Charge increase; and
WHEREAS, notice of the proposed Sewer Service Charge increase was mailed to ratepayers and property 
owners on April 13, 2023, 53 days prior to the scheduled public hearing on the proposed increase; and
WHEREAS, on June 5, 2023, a public hearing was held at 7:00 p.m. in the San Mateo City Hall Council 
Chambers, located at 330 West 20th Avenue, San Mateo regarding the proposed Sewer Service Charge increase, 
at which all protests against the rate increase and other comments were considered; and
WHEREAS, following the public hearing, it was determined that a majority protest did not exist; and
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.54.030 provides that the City Council shall establish the 
Sewer Service Charge by ordinance.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS:
Section 1. In accordance with California Constitution Article XIII D, Section 6, the City Council finds that:
(a) The revenues derived from the Sewer Service Charge do not exceed the funds required to provide 
sewer service because the rate increase is needed to fund ongoing sewer system operation and maintenance 
and meet capital expenditures.
(b) The revenues derived from the Sewer Service Charge will not be used for any purpose other than the 
provision of sewer service.
(c) The Sewer Service Charge will not exceed the cost of sewer service attributable to each parcel.
(d) The Sewer Service Charge is only charged for sewer service that is actually used by or currently 
available to the parcel in question.
(e) The Sewer Service Charge pays for the provision of sewer service and not for general governmental 
services.
Section 2. Pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.54.030, the maximum sewer service charge for Fiscal 
Years 2023-2024 through 2027-2028 is established as follows:

because the Sewer Service Charge is a funding mechanism for the City sewer operations and capital projects 
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project that may result in a potentially significant

invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and all other sections or 
portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect.

posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 2.15 of the City 
Charter.

adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective 30 days after its adoption.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report

Agenda Number:  22
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
Account Number: 10-2021
File ID: 23-7651
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Finance Department
MEETING DATE:
June 20, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Fiscal Year 2023-24 Operating and Capital Budgets and Gann Appropriations Limit Adoption and Fiscal Years 2023-28 
Capital Improvement Program – Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt Resolutions to approve the fiscal year 2023-24 operating and capital budgets, the 2023-24 position budget, the 
fiscal year 2023-24 Gann appropriations limit, and the fiscal years 2023-28 Capital Improvement Program.
BACKGROUND:
Prior to Council’s adoption of the 2023-24 budget on June 20, 2023, it holds two public hearings to receive public comment 
on the proposed budget and capital improvement program (CIP). Council was presented with a preview of the General 
Fund proposed 2023-24 budget on April 17, 2023 and held the first public hearing on June 5, 2023. In addition the 
Measure S Oversight Committee recommended that the City Council approve the Measure S-funded programs and capital 
improvement projects at its regular meeting on May 18, 2023. 
Overall, the proposed Citywide budget for 2023-24 is $317.5 million. This includes an operating budget of $239.1 million 
and a capital budget of $78.4 million. A summary of budget highlights is included in the City Manager’s Letter of Budget 
Transmittal in the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book, included as Attachment 4. This agenda report primarily focuses on the 
General Fund, as it accounts for major tax revenues and funds the City's core services. Additional narratives are provided 
for the Construction Services Fund, as it is a perennial major fund of the City’s operations, and the Sewer Fund, which is 
the City’s sole enterprise fund and the second largest fund of the City. Fund summaries for all other operating funds are 
provided in the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book. Narratives will be provided to discuss service-level enhancements as 
reflected in the 2023-24 position budget, but the focus of fiscal year (FY) 2023-24 is on the capital improvement program 
(CIP), as FY 2023-24 is year two of the two-year business plan. As such, the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book includes 
summaries for the 2023-28 CIP as well as charters for funded projects and priority unfunded projects. This agenda report 
provides a high-level summary. The Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book also includes operating budget schedules to provide 
greater detail.
GENERAL FUND 
The General Fund, which is the City's primary operating fund, projects total revenues of $159.1 million and total 
expenditures of $171.7 million and includes transfers out and all General Fund sub-funds – Measure S, Downtown 
Services, Recreation Services, and Golf. Budget assumptions for major General Fund tax revenues are discussed in greater 
detail below. 
181 of 536

--- page 182 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
GENERAL FUND REVENUES 
PROPERTY TAX 
The proposed 2022-23 budget anticipates $78.0 million in property tax revenues, which is 45% of General Fund total 
revenues, and as such, is the General Fund’s chief revenue source. The primary components of property tax revenues 
include secured current year ($49.2 million), Excess Educational Revenue Augmentation Fund (ERAF) ($8.2 million), and 
property tax in lieu of VLF ($10.8 million). These primary property tax components grow annually based on the change in 
assessed value, which as of May 2023, is projected to be 4.65%, which includes the change in the California Consumer 
Price Index (CCPI) or 2%, whichever is less, pursuant to the State constitution. The remainder of the growth in assessed 
value is attributable to properties that have been sold and the applicable assessed value reset to market value, as well as 
new development that has been added to the City’s property tax roll. The proposed 2023-24 budget anticipates that 
property tax will be $2.2 million less than the adopted 2022-23 budget. The primary culprit in the year-over-year reduction 
is property tax in-lieu of vehicle license fees (VLF). 
As a part of the 2004 budget agreement, the State of California agreed to swap the loss of vehicle license fees (otherwise 
known as VLF) with property tax. Property tax in-lieu of vehicle license fees has two funding sources: 1) property taxes 
from non-basic aid school districts, and 2) ERAF that would otherwise go to non-basic aid school districts. Due to the 
preponderance of basic aid school districts in the county (17 of 23), there are not enough non-basic aid school districts to 
backfill the VLF owed to the cities and the County. As such, the County of San Mateo has experienced property tax in lieu 
of VLF shortfalls over the past couple of years, which is an issue that is unique to San Mateo County. The procedure to 
address the VLF shortfall has been for the County to file a claim with the State at the end of the fiscal year of the VLF 
shortfall, which is taken into consideration by the State for the subsequent year’s budget. The county-wide VLF shortfall 
from fiscal year (FY) 2021-22 of $33 million is not included in the State’s proposed FY 2023-24 budget, despite advocacy 
efforts from San Mateo County and the local cities. The City’s share of the FY 2022-23 county-wide shortfall is $1.9 million. 
In addition, the proposed 2023-24 budget assumes a county-wide VLF shortfall of $78.5 million, of which the City’s share 
would be $4.6 million. Absent the 2021-22 VLF shortfall and the projected 2023-24 VLF shortfall, property tax revenues 
would be $84.0 million.
SALES TAX 
The proposed 2023-24 budget assumes Local 1% sales tax revenues (Bradley Burns) of $23.9 million and local 1/4% sales 
tax revenues (Measure S) of $7.3 million, which reflects modest growth of the City’s sales tax base after recovering back to 
the pre-pandemic baseline within a two-year period in FY 2021-22. Despite the relatively rapid recovery, the ongoing shift 
of consumer habits of purchasing taxable goods online rather than physical stores is expected to continue to keep sales tax 
growth at a modest pace, as the Bradley Burns is allocated based on point of sale. 
By contrast, Measure S, which is a 1/4% transactions and use tax, otherwise known as a district tax, is allocated based on 
destination. While the Bradley Burns and district tax are based on different tax laws, the disparity between the amount of 
revenue generated between the two sales tax revenues relative to their rate illustrates the impact of the gradual changes 
in consumer behavior. If the Bradley Burns tax were modified to be in alignment with modern technology and current 
consumer habits by being destination based, Bradley Burns sales tax revenues would be closer to $28.7 million rather than 
$23.5 million, a $5.2 million difference. 
REAL PROPERTY TRANSFER TAX 
The federal government’s efforts to mitigate high inflation by increasing the federal funds rate increases the cost to 
borrow money. These actions at the federal level have a stifling effect on the real estate market, which in turn has caused 
the City’s real property transfer tax (RPTT) to precipitously drop. The adopted 2022-23 budget assumed RPTT revenues of 
$11.1 million based on an analysis of RPTT trends over the current and previous economic cycles. As a result of the 
escalation of the federal funds rate, RPTT revenue for 2022-23 are projected to end the year at $6.3 million. Over the next 
couple of years, the Federal Open Market Committee anticipates that the federal funds rate will begin to taper off. As 
such, the proposed 2023-24 budget and the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan assume an average of $8.0 million 
throughout the 10-year period. The Letter of Transmittal in the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book discusses the current trend 
in greater detail.
182 of 536

--- page 183 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
TRANSIENT OCCUPANCY TAX
Local tourism and transient occupancy taxes (TOT) revenues have recovered faster than expected, but TOT remains the 
only tax revenue that has not yet recovered back to pre-pandemic levels. The proposed 2023-24 budget assumes that TOT 
revenues will reach $6.0 million. San Mateo’s TOT base is largely from business travel, which is anticipated to recover back 
to the pre-pandemic baseline by FY 2025-26 as reflected in the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan based on analysis 
from Tourism Economics and Visit California. 
GENERAL FUND EXPENDITURES 
The proposed 2023-24 budget projects $171.7 million in General Fund total expenditures, which primarily consists of $86.7 
million in employee compensation, which includes salaries, wages, healthcare, and pension costs. Employee compensation 
represents over half of total General Fund expenditures and reflects the City’s ongoing commitment to provide 
competitive employee compensation and the proportional demand on General Fund resources. FY 2023-24 is year two of 
the two-year business plan, so the focus of the budget is on the CIP. Modest service level enhancements added $1.4 
million in additional cost to the operating budget to address key operational areas, including planning, building, traffic, 
engineering, and wastewater, as detailed below:
Community Development Department
•
1.0 full-time equivalent (FTE) Principal Planner - $213,000; Allocated 25% to Advance Planning Fund and 75% 
Construction Services Fund
o
This position is needed to ensure the timely handling of the increased number of planning applications in 
accordance with State law, ensure the City achieves and remains in compliance with the high volume of 
new State land use laws, which expedite processing times and require policy and code changes, assist with 
and lead Housing Element and General Plan implementation tasks,  including the comprehensive Zoning 
Code update, and assist the Planning Manager with streamlining permit processing and supporting staff 
with handing current planning tasks.
•
1.0 FTE Deputy Building Official - $197,000; Allocated 10% to General Fund and 90% to Construction Services Fund
o
This position is needed to assist the Building Official with process improvements and help support staff on 
technical matters and complex customer service issues related to the approximately 4,000 permits 
processed each year, and which is increasing in volume and turnaround times commensurately with 
planning applications. The Building Division comprises approximately half of the Department’s staff and 
has not had a proportional amount of management support. Adding back this previously funded position 
into the organizational structure would provide the support needed to channel some of CDD’s resources 
toward staff mentorship and operational improvements to improve customer service and shorten permit 
processing and response times.
•
1.0 FTE Development Review Technician - $120,000; Allocated 100% to Construction Services Fund
o
This position is needed to field the high volume of building and planning inquiries we experience daily and 
process the intake of building and planning permits submitted, which continue to increase. Development 
Review Technicians are difficult to attract and retain, as compensation in nearby cities is competitive, 
while work volumes often appear to be lower. Further, firms that offer professional services often do not 
have staff available to fill this role, making it difficult to obtain help when there are unexpected vacancies 
in our DRT pool. Adding this position would provide for a more balanced workload when fully staffed, 
allowing us to focus on process improvements, and help to ensure customer levels can be maintained  
when we experience staffing shortages, which is not unusual for this position.
Public Works Department
•
1.0 FTE Traffic and Lighting Technician - $138,000; Allocated 100% to General Fund
o
The City has 90 traffic signals and 6,700 streetlights that are currently maintained by one Traffic and 
Lighting Technician and one Traffic Safety Worker. For traffic infrastructure of this size, the Federal 
Highway Administration (FHWA) recommends a staff of three Traffic and Lighting Technicians and two 
Traffic Safety Workers. While still short of the FHWA recommendations, an additional Traffic and Lighting 
Technician would help address the maintenance requirements to ensure the safe operation of the traffic 
infrastructure.  
183 of 536

--- page 184 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
•
1.0 FTE Associate Engineer - $168,000; Allocated 65% to General Fund and 35% to Construction Services Fund
o
The Traffic Engineering group within Public Works is responsible for improving the operation of the City’s 
traffic infrastructure through the optimization of traffic signal timing for congestion relief; addressing 
safety concerns through the development of speed studies and the implementation of traffic calming 
projects; review of collision data for the development of traffic safety improvements; development of 
capital improvement projects such as the 101/Peninsula Interchange, Speed Limit Zone Reductions, High 
Voltage, and 19th Ave. / Fashion Island Traffic Improvements; and review of traffic impact analyses for new 
development.  In addition, the group is responsible for leading studies, such as the Local Roadway Safety 
Plan, Traffic Signal Master Plan, and the Circulation Element of the General Plan. The group consists of one 
Senior Engineer, two Associate Engineers, an Engineering Technician and is resource limited, unable to 
address resident requests and the implementation of critical safety projects in a timely and efficient 
manner. Additional resources are needed to implement critical traffic calming and safety projects.  
•
1.0 FTE Pump Station Mechanic - $134,000; Allocated 70% to Sewer Fund and 30% to General Fund
o
The City has a total of 27 sewer and 10 storm pump stations that are currently operated and maintained 
by three Pump Station Mechanics and a Laborer. Ensuring that these critical facilities are properly 
maintained and operated requires additional staff, particularly during wet weather events. Many facilities 
do not have backup power and require the use of portable generators, which are rotated between stations 
to maintain the sewer system from backing up and creating sanitary sewer overflows. The storm pump 
stations require constant clearing of trash to avoid malfunction and flooding. With an additional staff, 
preventive maintenance measures can be more effectively implemented and response times during 
emergencies greatly improved. 
•
1.0 FTE Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) Operator I/II - $139,000; Allocated 100% to Sewer Fund
o
With the expansion of the WWTP, a staff derivation study was conducted to determine the adequate 
number of staff needed to operate the facility. An additional operator is needed to ensure that the facility 
is well monitored and operated to meet regulatory requirements. 
Parks & Recreation Department
The Recreation Division is reorganizing based on current programming needs.  With recent vacancies, the department has 
an opportunity to reallocate resources into more front-line support positions and reduce overhead management positions.  
With this proposed reorganization, the department will eliminate vacancies in the following areas:  the community 
services manager, two supervisor positions, and two lead teacher positions.  
In addition, a section manager position in the Police Activity League (PAL) will be downgraded to a supervisor role. This 
change is due to elimination of funding from PAL for the additional cost of the section manager. 
Those vacancies will allow the addition of four new coordinator positions and one new senior program assistant position 
and the reclassification of a program assistant position to a senior program assistant position.  This structure will allow the 
department to create better workflow, delegation and oversight of work projects and daily tasks, along with creating a 
more streamlined succession plan for long-term outlook and recruitment.  This will allow for better service to the public 
and for streamlining of business operations.  Funding for these changes will be close to budget neutral. These changes are 
subject to bargaining unit approval, which will be in process shortly. 
NON-PERSONNEL OPERATING BUDGET ADDITIONS
The proposed 2023-24 budget includes $1.5 million in additions to the operating budget; $1.0 million of which is 
attributable to the General Fund. New additions of $50,000 or more to the operating budget are noted below by 
department:
Police Department
•
Security improvements to property and evidence room - $60,000
o
Funding is intended to address security and organizational concerns identified through a recent audit of 
the Police Department property and evidence room. The purpose is to enhance the digital locker system, 
upgrade storage systems for firearms and currency, as well as replace out of date non-ergonomic stations 
184 of 536

--- page 185 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
and evidence processing tables. Funds will also be designated for implementation of a more uniform 
storage system for existing evidence, reducing the chance of lost or damaged items stored for an extended 
period.
•
Additional cost for digital evidence software - $93,000
o
With the ever-evolving world of technology, upgrades to forensic tools and equipment are necessary. This 
funding will be used to enhance the San Mateo Digital Forensic Extraction Team’s ability to perform full-
file system and physical extractions from computers and cellular phones with the Cellebrite Premium 
Service. This new software will assist in accessing highly protected areas such as the iOS Keychain or the 
Secure Folder. Accessing third party application data, stored passwords and tokens, chat conversations, 
location data, email attachments, system logs, as well as deleted content, will increase the unit’s chances 
of finding the incriminating evidence.
•
Police Department staffing study - $100,000 (one-time)
o
The study is expected to result in the creation of a report outlining current capabilities and future needs of 
the Police Department based on growth and development within the City. The report will include the 
current status of the agency and actionable steps to improve, remodel, build or increase facilities, 
personnel, and infrastructure.
Public Works Department
•
Additional street sweeper to address downtime associated with ongoing vehicle problems and reliability - 
$410,000 (one-time)
•

•

monitoring and control modifications, diagnostics, and other general needs - $80,000

(SCADA)/Wonderware - $50,000
•
Anticipated increases to cleaning/sweeping services for the Downtown Kiku garage; current contract budgeted at 
$500k/yr - $81,000
PENSION CONTRIBUTIONS
A key component of the cost of employee compensation is the unfunded accrued liability (UAL) with the California Public 
Employees’ Retirement System (CalPERS). The UAL is the amortized cost of CalPERS’ investment gains/losses as well as 
changes in actuarial assumptions; in other words - a loan from CalPERS to the City paid back at 7.0% interest over 30 years 
for investment gains/losses on or before June 30, 2019 and 20 years for investment gains/losses after June 30, 2019. For 
FY 2023-24, the total UAL is $17.6 million; the General Fund’s share of which is $13.5 million. As reflected in the General 
Fund Long-Term Financial Plan, the General Fund’s share of the UAL will precipitously increase to $18.7 million in 2024-25 
and as high as $24.6 million in 2030-31 as a result of CalPERS’ negative 7.5% investment return for the fiscal year ended 
June 30, 2022. Over the course of the 20-year amortization period, CalPERS’ investment loss from June 30, 2022 will 
require the City to pay $177 million more in pension contributions. The escalating cost of pension contributions continues 
to reduce the City’s capacity to address competing demands with its limited resources, including service level 
enhancements and unfunded infrastructure needs.
Transfers Out | Investment in Infrastructure and Capital Improvements
The General Fund supports other needs by transferring resources out to other funds. For fiscal year 2023-24, total 
transfers out are projected to be $14.2 million:
•
$7.25 million to support the City’s capital improvement plan (CIP), which is $3.0 million more than the General 
Fund has historically contributed to the CIP, which is indicative of the City Council’s commitment to continue 
investing in the City’s infrastructure and capital improvements.
•
$3.85 million of Measure S funds to support the CIP;
•
$2.69 million from Measure S to transfer out for debt service related to flood control and street rehabilitation; and
•
$0.41 million to address building maintenance needs
185 of 536

--- page 186 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
General Fund Long-Term Plan
Over the next 10 years, the General Fund is projected to draw down Rainy Day Reserves to as low as $15 million in 2030-31 
and recover to $25.7 million by 2032-33. Key revenue and expenditure assumptions are noted below:
GENERAL FUND REVENUES:
•
The State of California will continue to honor the escalating VLF shortfall claims. As the number of non-basic aid 
school districts continues to diminish, the VLF shortfall will continue to grow. If every school district reaches basic 
aid status in 10 years, the county-wide VLF shortfall could reach upwards of $370 million by 2032-33, of which the 
City’s share would be over $20 million. At present, the State of California’s 2023-24 May 2023 Revised Budget does 
not include appropriations to address San Mateo County’s $33 million VLF shortfall. If the State no longer honors 
the annual VLF shortfall claim or does not make a legislative amendment to address this issue, the City’s revenue 
loss is projected to be over $100 million through 2032-33.
•
Inflation and the Federal Fund Rate will reach 3.0% by 2025-26. These metrics have a direct nexus to the interest 
rates available for purchasing homes, and by extension, the City’s real property transfer tax. 
•
The majority of the City’s TOT base is from business travel, which is expected to return to the 2018-19 baseline by 
2025-26 based on projections from Tourism Economics and Visit California.   
•
If the projections for General Fund revenues and expenditures come to fruition, the General Fund would realize a 
net loss of $12.6 million for 2023-24. As shown in the General Fund Long-Term Plan, based on revenue and 
expenditure assumptions, the City’s Rainy Day Reserve is projected to reach a low of $16.0 million in 2031 before 
recovering to the target reserve policy goal of $30.0 million by 2033.
•
Policy H1.2 of the City’s Housing Element calls for the City to resume contributing 20% of the property tax that the 
City receives as a taxing entity from the former Redevelopment Agency (RDA), also known as ”boomerang funds” 
to the housing set aside in fiscal year 2023-24, which the City can use for affordable housing developments. The 
City suspended its practice due to the immediate and severe financial impact of COVID-19 in 2020. For 2023-24, 
this would result in $1.0 million shifting from the Rainy Day Reserve to the housing set aside. 
GENERAL FUND EXPENDITURES
•
Employee cost of living adjustments (COLA) will keep pace with long-term inflation at 2%.
•
Pension contributions will increase sharply starting in 2024-25 due to CalPERS’ investment losses from 2021-22. 
Over the 20-year amortization period starting in 2024-25, the City will pay $177 million more in pension 
contributions. The General Fund Long-Term Plan assumes that CalPERS will meet its target rate of return (discount 
rate) for the remainder of the 10-year forecast. 
NET REDUCTIONS
Net Reductions reflect potential favorable deviations from revenue or expenditure assumptions. For example, the City’s 
long-term assumption for the increase in AV for property tax is 4%. In any given year, if the AV is greater than the long-
term assumption, the City would recognize additional revenue. Conversely, the City’s vacancy assumption for non-safety 
positions is 2%. If the actual vacancy rate is greater in a fiscal year, then the expenditures would be less than projected. 
The General Fund Long-Term Plan assumes net reductions of $3.1 million to $3.7 million each year.
The chart below illustrates the budget to actual deviations that have come to fruition over the past five fiscal years:

Transportation Improvement Fee and The General Plan
The City’s Municipal Code Chapter 27.13 established a transportation improvement fee (TIF) for new development to help 
fund transportation improvements necessary to maintain intersection improvements that will accommodate future 
growth.  The TIF currently funds transportation improvement projects that are necessary to maintain consistency with the 
General Plan Policy (C2.1), which requires that intersections not exceed a vehicle-specific level of service or a certain 
amount of delay. Policy C2.1 is changing with the forthcoming General Plan Update. As such, the list of projects that are 
required to accommodate future growth will change in order to be consistent with the new General Plan policy. Once the 
General Plan is adopted, staff will develop a new list of traffic improvements that aligns with the updated General Plan 
policy, and then update the TIF. Future transportation improvement projects will be programmed into the capital 
improvement plan once this effort is completed.
The narratives below list each project type, the total CIP cost over the five-year period, and major projects within each 
project type for the 2023-28 CIP. The proposed 2023-24 budget book includes more detailed information on the 2023-28 
CIP, including a charter for new funded projects as well as priority unfunded projects.

CONSTRUCTION SERVICES FUND 
The Construction Services Fund accounts for the revenues and expenditures of the building permit activities. Total 2023-24 
revenues are projected to be $8.8 million, total expenditures are projected to be $12.5 million, and transfers out are 
projected to be $5.5 million. If the proposed budget estimates come to fruition, the Construction Services Fund will result 
in a planned draw on fund balance of $9.2 million, bringing the fund balance closer to the reserve policy of 12 months of 
190 of 536

--- page 191 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
operating expenditures. 
SEWER FUND 
The proposed 2023-24 budget projects $86.5 million in revenues, principally comprised of sewer service fees of $80.6 
million. The projected revenues reflect the anticipated annual rate increases of 3% for fiscal years 2023-24 through 2027-
28 as proposed in a separate agenda item to support the capital improvements and maintain ongoing services. Total 
expenses are projected to be $230.1 million, comprised of $28.5 million in operating expenses, $173.6 million in CIP, and 
$28.1 million in debt service. CIP expenses will largely be funded by the low-interest loans from the state and federal 
government and primarily reflects the commitment to complete the Clean Water Program, a comprehensive plan to 
update the wastewater collection system and wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).
 
GANN LIMIT
In addition to the adoption of the 2023-24 budget and capital improvement program, there will be one other action 
requested of Council as part of the overall budget adoption process on June 20th. This action is to establish the City’s 
appropriations limit for 2023-24. The appropriations limit, which was originally established in 1979 by Proposition 4, places 
a maximum limit of the appropriation of tax proceeds that can be made by the State, school districts, and local 
governments in California. California Government Code requires that the City annually adopt an appropriations limit for 
the upcoming fiscal year. The appropriations limit for the City of San Mateo for 2023-24 is $260,445,497, while the 
proceeds of taxes subject to the appropriations limit is $135,537,117. Therefore, the City is well below its appropriations 
limit for fiscal year 2023-24.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The overall proposed 2023-24 budget is $317.5 million, comprised of $239.1 million for the operating budget and $78.4 
million for the CIP. The General Fund is projected to account for $159.1 million in revenues and $171.7 million in 
expenditures, reflecting an anticipated draw on the Rainy Day Reserve due to front-loading support for the CIP and the 
impact of the VLF shortfall. The Construction Services Fund is expected to have $8.8 million in revenues and $12.5 million 
in expenditures for a planned draw on reserves of $3.7 million. The Sewer Fund is expected to account for $86.5 million in 
revenues and $230.1 million in expenses, largely comprised of CIP for the Clean Water Program that is primarily funded by 
low-interest loans from the state and federal government.
The 2023-28 CIP is projected to be $284.4 million. Each fiscal year, the City Council only appropriates funds for Year One of 
the five-year CIP. The 2023-24 CIP is projected to be $78.4 million.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This budget adoption is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

ADOPTING THE 2023-24 CITY BUDGET
WHEREAS, the City Council held public hearings on the Budget and Capital Improvement Program, as 
required by the City Charter section 5.02; and
WHEREAS, budget required to operate and support the City departments and to pay the bonded 
indebtedness of the City, or any portion or district therein, is $239,083,971 for Operating and $78,032,026 for 
the Capital Improvement Program (CIP) for fiscal year July 1, 2023 to June 30, 2024; and
WHEREAS, Municipal Code Section 3.50.060, Fiscal Accountability Provisions, requires that the amount 
of revenue generated by the one-quarter cent Measure S Transactions and Use Tax and how it was used be 
included in the annual financial audit; and
WHEREAS, budgeted resources and requirements are sufficient to meet Council’s adopted financial 
policy that the General Fund reserve will be maintained in an amount equal to at least three months, or 25 
percent, of the annual General Fund operating expenditure budget to be used in the event of significant 
financial emergency. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, RESOLVES that:

which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially 
significant physical impact on the environment.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. _____ (2023)
APPROVING THE 2023-2028 CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM 
WHEREAS, the City Council held public hearings on the Budget and Capital Improvement Program (CIP), 
as required by the City Charter section 5.02 ; and
WHEREAS, the CIP is a five-year plan that identifies all planned capital improvement projects, their 
estimated cost, and a revenue and/or funding source forecast; and
WHEREAS, the 2023-28 Capital Budget presented as part of the proposed budget document that was 
delivered to Council on June 5, 2023 included details on recommended and funded CIP projects, as well as 
projects without an identified funding source; and
WHEREAS, the 2023-2028 CIP includes capital projects planned for the five-year period totaling 
$284,360,476; and
WHEREAS, no changes to the 2023-2028 CIP were proposed by the Council; and
WHEREAS, section 5.09 of the City’s Charter requires that the CIP be approved by Council resolution. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, RESOLVES that:
1.
In accordance California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines section 15378(b)(4), 
adoption of the Capital Improvement Program is exempt from CEQA because it is a government 
fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in 
a potentially significant physical impact on the environment.
2.
The 2023-2028 Capital Improvement Program for the City of San Mateo, set forth in the 
accompanying Administrative Report and the proposed business plan document, is approved.
199 of 536

--- page 200 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO 
RESOLUTION NO. ______ (2023) 
 
ESTABLISHING THE CITY’S APPROPRIATIONS LIMIT FOR FISCAL YEAR 2023-24
 
 
 
WHEREAS, under Article XIII B of the California Constitution and Government Code sections 7900 and 
following, the City is required to establish an annual appropriations limit based on revenues obtained from the 
proceeds of taxes; and 
 
 
WHEREAS, the method and basis of calculating these limits was revised by Proposition 111, amending 
Article XIII B of the State Constitution, and the implementing legislation, to allow election of the basis for 
population adjustment between the change in population in either the city or the county and an election of 
inflation factor between the growth of California per capita income or the growth of nonresidential assessed 
valuation due to new construction in the city; and 
 
 
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo population percentage change over the prior year is negative 0.32 
percent and the growth in California per capita personal income is 4.44 percent;  
 
 
WHEREAS, utilizing the population growth for the City of San Mateo and the growth in California per 
capital personal income yield the most favorable appropriations limit; and
WHEREAS, the appropriations limit for 2023-24 is $260,445,497 and the estimated budgeted proceeds 
from taxes are $135,537,117. 
 
  
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, RESOLVES, that: 
 
1.
In accordance with Public Resources Code Section 21065, this action is exempt from review 
under the California Environmental Quality Act because it can be seen with certainty that it will not have a 
significant impact on the environment.
2.
The City Council finds that the adjustment factors shall be based on the City’s population growth 
and the growth in California per capita income to determine the appropriations limit for the fiscal year 2023-24. 
 
3.
The City Council approves the appropriation limit on revenue obtained from the proceeds of 
taxes for fiscal year 2023-24 to be $260,445,497 using the selected adjustment factors.

Transmittal of Proposed Budget
City Manager's Letter of Transmittal
Honorable Mayor Lee and Members of the City Council:
The City’s long-term fiscal condition is healthy due to prudent fiscal discipline that the City Council exercised beginning in 
the wake of the Great Recession to accumulate resources in preparation for the next economic fluctuation. The landscape 
of the City’s post-COVID economic recovery reflects a diametrically opposed impact of inflation on major tax revenues. 
While inflation largely spurred the relatively rapid recovery of sales tax revenues back to the pre-pandemic baseline within 
two years, it is also, by extension, unfavorably impacting real property transfer tax (RPTT) revenues due to the high cost to 
borrow money.  
In contrast to the rapid recovery of sales tax revenues, transient occupancy tax revenues (TOT) have yet to recover back to 
the pre-pandemic baseline. However, the recovery trend is better than previously projected. TOT revenues are projected to 
end fiscal year 2022-23 at $5.0 million, which is 71% of the pre-pandemic baseline, and largely reflect the recovery trends 
of business travel, which is the primary base for the City’s TOT revenues. 
While sales tax, RPTT, and TOT are susceptible to economic ebb and flow, the challenges with the property tax in-lieu of 
vehicle license fees (VLF) are due to a structural flaw that began with the swap of property tax with a reduction in vehicle 
license fees from 20 years ago as a part of the 2004 budget deal with the State of California. Due to the limited number of 
non-basic aid school districts in San Mateo County, a VLF shortfall has occurred each year since 2020-21 through present. 
The VLF shortfall issue will be discussed in greater detail later in this letter of transmittal. 
The City has prepared a two-year business plan since 1986, in which the focus of year one of the business plan is on the 
operating budget, and the focus of year two of the business plan is on the Capital Improvement Program (CIP). Due to the 
fiscal uncertainty of the pandemic, the City shifted to a one-year budget beginning in fiscal year 2020-21 and returned to 
the two-year business plan in fiscal year 2022-23. Fiscal year 2023-24 represents year two of the business plan, as such, 
the focus is on the CIP.  
2023-24 Budget and Long-Term Financial Plan Overview 
The proposed 2023-24 budget projects General Fund total revenues of $159.1 million and total expenditures of 
$171.7 million. Of note, the projection for total revenues reflects the State of California’s 2023-24 budget not including 
appropriations to address the 2021-22 county-wide VLF shortfall of $33 million. In addition, the proposed budget includes 
substantial support for the CIP to address the City’s infrastructure needs. 
The City’s General Fund is in a strong fiscal position with an estimated $111.9 million in reserves at the end of FY 2022-23, 
which includes $71.5 million in Rainy Day Reserves, which is above and beyond the 25% operating reserve of $37.3 million. 
In contrast to the City’s limited fiscal position during the Great Recession, City Council’s prudent fiscal discipline during the 
period of recovery and growth post-Great Recession positioned the General Fund to be able to draw on the Rainy Day 
Reserves during economic fluctuations. The Rainy Day Reserves are expected to decline from a projected high of $71.5 
million in 2023 to a low of $21.0 million in 2031 and build to the policy goal of $30.0 million in 2033.
Of note, there are two major assumptions in the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan: 1) that the State of California will 
honor future VLF shortfall claims; and 2) that the California Public Employees’ Retirement System’s (CalPERS) investment 
earnings will meet its discount rate. If CalPERS’ investment earnings unfavorably deviate from the discount rate, amortized 
investment losses will further diminish the City’s long-term fiscal position.
2

Transmittal of Proposed Budget
The total City operating budget for 2023-24 is $239.1 million and is up 10.2% increase over the adopted 2022-23 budget. 
The cost escalation is primarily due to capitalized interest from the 2021 Notes, which were issued as a part of the Plan of 
Finance for the Clean Water Program in a prior fiscal year. Absent the capitalized interest, the operating budget increased 
by 3.9% due to the cost of employee compensation, reflecting negotiated compensation increases and escalation in the 
cost of healthcare and pension contributions. Employee compensation (Salaries and Wages, Benefits, and 86.7% of the 
operating contribution to San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department) represents over 60% of the total operating budget, 
which reflects the cost to provide core services to the San Mateo community. 
The CIP for 2023-24 totals $78.0 million, which is $10.1 million more than the adopted 2022-23 CIP, and $16.0 million less 
than the 2023-24 CIP estimate from the adopted 2022-23 CIP. The change in CIP for 2023-24 between the prior 5-year CIP 
and the proposed 2023-24 CIP was primarily in the Sanitary Sewer project type, which shifted some planned project costs 
to 2025-26. Nearly half of the proposed 2023-24 CIP budget is for Sewer Fund capital improvements, and the majority 
of which are for the Clean Water Program annual citywide sanitary sewer rehabilitation ($11.7 million) and Wastewater 
Treatment Plant (WWTP) Flow Management Upgrade and Expansion ($11.2 million) project. 
2023-24 Proposed Budget
With the above recovery trends and challenges in context, I am pleased to present the 2023-24 budget. Overall, the 
proposed citywide budget, including both operating and capital, is $317.1 million. 
The City’s proposed budget for 2023-24 continues to provide for core services, Measure S initiatives, and Council priorities, 
while also making a substantial investment in our capital and infrastructure needs. The following table summarizes the 
proposed spending plans for operating and capital expenditures for 2023-24.
